* [Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin") * [Jonny Buckland](/wiki/Jonny_Buckland "Jonny Buckland") * [Guy Berryman](/wiki/Guy_Berryman "Guy Berryman") * [Will Champion](/wiki/Will_Champion "Will Champion") * [Phil Harvey](/wiki/Phil_Harvey_(band_manager) "Phil Harvey (band manager)")

are a British [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band formed in London in 1997. They consist of vocalist and pianist [Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin"), guitarist [Jonny Buckland](/wiki/Jonny_Buckland "Jonny Buckland"), bassist [Guy Berryman](/wiki/Guy_Berryman "Guy Berryman") and drummer [Will Champion](/wiki/Will_Champion "Will Champion"); manager [Phil Harvey](/wiki/Phil_Harvey_(band_manager) "Phil Harvey (band manager)") is also credited as a full member. The group are best known for [their live performances](/wiki/List_of_Coldplay_live_performances "List of Coldplay live performances") and have had a significant [impact on popular culture](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay "Cultural impact of Coldplay").

first_paragraph: **Coldplay** are a British [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band formed in London in 1997. They consist of vocalist and pianist [Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin"), guitarist [Jonny Buckland](/wiki/Jonny_Buckland "Jonny Buckland"), bassist [Guy Berryman](/wiki/Guy_Berryman "Guy Berryman") and drummer [Will Champion](/wiki/Will_Champion "Will Champion"); manager [Phil Harvey](/wiki/Phil_Harvey_(band_manager) "Phil Harvey (band manager)") is also credited as a full member. The group are best known for [their live performances](/wiki/List_of_Coldplay_live_performances "List of Coldplay live performances") and have had a significant [impact on popular culture](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay "Cultural impact of Coldplay").

**Coldplay** are a British [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") band formed in London in 1997. They consist of vocalist and pianist [Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin"), guitarist [Jonny Buckland](/wiki/Jonny_Buckland "Jonny Buckland"), bassist [Guy Berryman](/wiki/Guy_Berryman "Guy Berryman") and drummer [Will Champion](/wiki/Will_Champion "Will Champion"); manager [Phil Harvey](/wiki/Phil_Harvey_(band_manager) "Phil Harvey (band manager)") is also credited as a full member. The group are best known for [their live performances](/wiki/List_of_Coldplay_live_performances "List of Coldplay live performances") and have had a significant [impact on popular culture](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay "Cultural impact of Coldplay").

Coldplay initially went by the names Big Fat Noises and Starfish. Following the independent release of the EP *[Safety](/wiki/Safety_(EP) "Safety (EP)")* (1998), the band signed a record deal with [Parlophone](/wiki/Parlophone "Parlophone") in 1999 and issued their debut album, *[Parachutes](/wiki/Parachutes_(Coldplay_album) "Parachutes (Coldplay album)")* (2000), which included the breakthrough single "[Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_(Coldplay_song) "Yellow (Coldplay song)")"; the album won a [Brit Award for British Album of the Year](/wiki/Brit_Award_for_British_Album_of_the_Year "Brit Award for British Album of the Year") and a [Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Alternative_Music_Album "Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album"). The follow-up, *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")* (2002), earned the same accolades. *[X&Y](/wiki/X%26Y "X&Y")* (2005) concluded what Coldplay viewed as a trilogy, and their fourth album, *[Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")* (2008), won a [Grammy Award for Best Rock Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Rock_Album "Grammy Award for Best Rock Album"); both releases topped the charts in over 30 countries, becoming the [best-sellers of their respective years](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums#Best-selling_album_by_year_worldwide "List of best-selling albums") worldwide. *Viva la Vida*'s [title track](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida "Viva la Vida") was the first song by a British act to reach number one in the United States and United Kingdom simultaneously in the 21st century.

The albums *[Mylo Xyloto](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto "Mylo Xyloto")* (2011), *[Ghost Stories](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_(Coldplay_album) "Ghost Stories (Coldplay album)")* (2014), *[A Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams "A Head Full of Dreams")* (2015), *[Everyday Life](/wiki/Everyday_Life_(Coldplay_album) "Everyday Life (Coldplay album)")* (2019), *[Music of the Spheres](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_(Coldplay_album) "Music of the Spheres (Coldplay album)")* (2021) and *[Moon Music](/wiki/Moon_Music "Moon Music")* (2024) drew from genres such as [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B"), [ambient](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music"), [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco"), [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") and [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock"). *Music of the Spheres* included the single "[My Universe](/wiki/My_Universe_(song) "My Universe (song)")", which was the first song by a British group to debut at number one on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"), and the band's [Music of the Spheres World Tour](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Music of the Spheres World Tour") is the [most-attended](/wiki/List_of_most-attended_concert_tours "List of most-attended concert tours") and [second-highest-grossing tour of all time](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_concert_tours "List of highest-grossing concert tours"), becoming the first by a band to gross $1 billion. A documentary, *[Coldplay: A Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/Coldplay:_A_Head_Full_of_Dreams "Coldplay: A Head Full of Dreams")*, was released in 2018 for Coldplay's 20th anniversary. In 2023, they featured on the inaugural [*Time* 100 Climate](/wiki/Time_100#Time_100_Climate "Time 100") list.

With over 160 million records sold worldwide, Coldplay are one of the [best-selling music acts of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists"). They are also the first group in [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") history to reach 100 million [monthly listeners](/wiki/List_of_most-streamed_artists_on_Spotify "List of most-streamed artists on Spotify"). [Fuse](/wiki/Fuse_(TV_channel) "Fuse (TV channel)") listed them among the [most awarded artists](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Coldplay "List of awards and nominations received by Coldplay"), which includes the record for most [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards") won by a band. In the United Kingdom, they have three of the 50 [best-selling albums](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of best-selling albums in the United Kingdom"), the most [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart") number ones without missing the top (10),[[1]](#cite_note-1) and are the most played group of the 21st century on British media. The [British Phonographic Industry](/wiki/British_Phonographic_Industry "British Phonographic Industry") called them one of the world's most "influential and pioneering acts",[[2]](#cite_note-2) while the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") added *A Rush of Blood to the Head* to the [200 Definitive Albums](/wiki/List_of_200_Definitive_Albums_in_the_Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "List of 200 Definitive Albums in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") list and "Yellow" to the *Songs That Shaped Rock and Roll* exhibit.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Coldplay are involved in philanthropy, politics and activism, supporting numerous humanitarian projects and donating 10% of their profits to charity. Despite their popularity, they are considered [polarising cultural icons](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay#Public_image "Cultural impact of Coldplay").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1997–1999: Formation and first years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1997–1999: Formation and first years")]

> The final name change came courtesy of close friend and fellow [UCL](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") student Tim Crompton. Tim was in the process of putting a band of old schoolmates together (later called Bettina Motive). While killing time waiting for a delayed removal van, he had considered the name after he found a copy of Philip Horky's book, *Child's Reflections, Cold Play*. Tim had a list of potential band names but Cold Play was quickly rejected; the future bandmates didn't like it, so they discarded it. Starfish were happy to pick it up.

— *Life in Technicolor: A Celebration of Coldplay*, 2018[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin") and [Jonny Buckland](/wiki/Jonny_Buckland "Jonny Buckland") met during their first week at [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London"), in September 1996.[[6]](#cite_note-6) They began to write their first songs together in early 1997.[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Guy Berryman](/wiki/Guy_Berryman "Guy Berryman") was the third to join the band months later, and the trio recorded numerous demos without a drummer, calling themselves Big Fat Noises by November.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In January 1998, [Will Champion](/wiki/Will_Champion "Will Champion") joined.[[9]](#cite_note-WCJRFC-9) He said that Martin, Buckland and Berryman came to his house because his roommate was a good drummer with a drum kit; however, when the roommate did not arrive, Champion played instead.[[9]](#cite_note-WCJRFC-9)

Champion scheduled the band's live debut a few days later, on 16 January 1998 at the Laurel Tree in [Camden](/wiki/Camden_Town "Camden Town").[[10]](#cite_note-TLTS-10) They had not chosen a proper name yet and called themselves Starfish.[[10]](#cite_note-TLTS-10) After settling on the name Coldplay, they released the EP *[Safety](/wiki/Safety_(EP) "Safety (EP)")* in May, financed by Martin's lifelong friend [Phil Harvey](/wiki/Phil_Harvey_(band_manager) "Phil Harvey (band manager)").[[11]](#cite_note-TCR-11) 150 out of 500 copies went to open market.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Harvey sold the first one to his roommate for £3 and the rest were given to record labels.[[13]](#cite_note-WIPH-13)

As Martin complained about the "vice-like grip" one of the Camden promoters had on the band, Harvey suggested they book their own concert at [Dingwalls](/wiki/Dingwalls "Dingwalls"); there, they sold 50 copies of *Safety*.[[13]](#cite_note-WIPH-13) The event is generally considered when Harvey officially became Coldplay's manager, and he eventually dropped out of his course at [Trinity College, Oxford](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Oxford "Trinity College, Oxford"), to dedicate himself to the role.[[11]](#cite_note-TCR-11)[[13]](#cite_note-WIPH-13) More concerts were scheduled for the summer.[[14]](#cite_note-CHERRY-14) Martin tried to invite [Tim Rice-Oxley](/wiki/Tim_Rice-Oxley "Tim Rice-Oxley") to play keyboard for the band, but this was vetoed by the other band members.[[14]](#cite_note-CHERRY-14) In September 1998, Coldplay performed at Manchester's In the City showcase, where they were discovered by A&R scout Debs Wild.[[15]](#cite_note-15) *Safety* was followed by a cassette demo with "Ode to Deodorant" and "[Brothers & Sisters](/wiki/Brothers_%26_Sisters_(song) "Brothers & Sisters (song)")".[[16]](#cite_note-DWI-16)

Wild informed [BMG Music Publishing](/wiki/Bertelsmann_Music_Group#BMG_Music_Publishing "Bertelsmann Music Group")'s Caroline Elleray and lawyer Gavin Maude about the group.[[16]](#cite_note-DWI-16) Elleray then talked to Dan Keeling at [Parlophone](/wiki/Parlophone "Parlophone"), but he had already passed on them.[[16]](#cite_note-DWI-16) Maude talked to Simon Williams from [Fierce Panda](/wiki/Fierce_Panda_Records "Fierce Panda Records"), who contacted the BBC DJ [Steve Lamacq](/wiki/Steve_Lamacq "Steve Lamacq").[[16]](#cite_note-DWI-16) On 3 January 1999, Coldplay became the first unsigned act to appear on Lamacq's *Evening Session*.[[17]](#cite_note-17) A month later, they signed a short-term contract with Fierce Panda and re-recorded "Brothers & Sisters".[[18]](#cite_note-EAK-18) Six labels offered a contract as the band's popularity grew, but they wanted Parlophone, which led Elleray to meet Keeling again.[[18]](#cite_note-EAK-18) He changed his mind, and the deal was signed at [Trafalgar Square](/wiki/Trafalgar_Square "Trafalgar Square") in April 1999, the same month in which "Brothers & Sisters" was released.[[18]](#cite_note-EAK-18) The band spent the following days studying for UCL finals.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

On 27 June 1999, Coldplay made their first appearance at the [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival") in the New Bands Tent.[[20]](#cite_note-20) They later recorded *[The Blue Room](/wiki/The_Blue_Room_(EP) "The Blue Room (EP)")*, which had 5,000 copies pressed and sold to the public.[[21]](#cite_note-ROACH28-21) Its sessions were originally meant for *[Parachutes](/wiki/Parachutes_(Coldplay_album) "Parachutes (Coldplay album)")* (2000), but became tumultuous due to Martin having heated discussions regarding Champion's abilities as a drummer.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Martin said that "Three days later, the rest of us were feeling miserable and [...] we asked him to come back. They made me have lots of vodka and cranberry juice in remembrance of what a nasty piece of work I was being. Now if I find myself making a big mistake, I have to force myself to drink that stuff".[[23]](#cite_note-23) After working out their differences, the band chose to operate as a democracy, established a new set of rules and proclaimed that anyone using [hard drugs](/wiki/Recreational_drug_use#Drug_harmfulness "Recreational drug use") would be fired immediately, inspired by [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.") and [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### 2000–2001: *Parachutes*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2000–2001: Parachutes")]

The band first planned to record their debut album, *Parachutes*, over the span of two weeks. However, due to tours and other live performances, the recording took place between September 1999 and May 2000.[[25]](#cite_note-ROACH45-25) The album was initially recorded at [Rockfield Studios](/wiki/Rockfield_Studios "Rockfield Studios"), Matrix Studios and [Wessex Sound Studios](/wiki/Wessex_Sound_Studios "Wessex Sound Studios") with producer [Ken Nelson](/wiki/Ken_Nelson_(British_record_producer) "Ken Nelson (British record producer)"), although the majority of *Parachutes*' tracks were recorded at [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool")'s Parr Street Studios (where they used three studio rooms). American engineer [Michael Brauer](/wiki/Michael_Brauer "Michael Brauer") in New York mixed all of the songs for the album.[[26]](#cite_note-26) During that time, they played on the [Carling Tour](/wiki/NME_Tours "NME Tours"), which showcased up-and-coming acts.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![Coldplay performing "Yellow" live in 2005](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/YellowLive.JPG/250px-YellowLive.JPG)](/wiki/File:YellowLive.JPG)

Coldplay performing "[Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_(Coldplay_song) "Yellow (Coldplay song)")", their breakthrough hit, from the band's 2000 debut album *[Parachutes](/wiki/Parachutes_(Coldplay_album) "Parachutes (Coldplay album)")*, in 2005

After releasing two EPs without a hit song, Coldplay had their first [Top 40](/wiki/Top_40 "Top 40") hit with the lead single from *Parachutes*, "[Shiver](/wiki/Shiver_(Coldplay_song) "Shiver (Coldplay song)")", which was released in March 2000, the same week Coldplay played [The Forum in Tunbridge Wells](/wiki/The_Forum,_Tunbridge_Wells "The Forum, Tunbridge Wells") supporting the band Terris as part of the NME Premier Tour.[[28]](#cite_note-28) "Shiver" peaked at the number 35 position on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart").[[29]](#cite_note-29) June 2000 was a pivotal moment in Coldplay's history; they embarked on their first headlining tour, including a performance at the [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival"). They also released the single "[Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_(Coldplay_song) "Yellow (Coldplay song)")";[[30]](#cite_note-ROACH42-30) it was Coldplay's first release to reach the top five and rose to number four on the UK Singles Chart.[[30]](#cite_note-ROACH42-30) The minimalistic music video for "Yellow" was filmed at [Studland Bay](/wiki/Studland_Bay "Studland Bay") in Dorset and featured Martin singing the song in one continuous shot as he walked along the beach.[[31]](#cite_note-31) "Yellow" and "Shiver" were initially released as EPs in the spring of 2000.[[32]](#cite_note-32) "Yellow" was released as a single in the United Kingdom on 26 June 2000. In the United States, it was released as the lead single from the then-untitled debut album. In October 2000, the track was sent to American college and alternative radio outlets.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Coldplay released *Parachutes* on 10 July 2000 in the United Kingdom via their record label, [Parlophone](/wiki/Parlophone_Records "Parlophone Records"). The album debuted at number one on the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart").[[25]](#cite_note-ROACH45-25) It was released on 7 November 2000 by record label [Nettwerk](/wiki/Nettwerk "Nettwerk") in North America.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The album has been made available in various formats since its initial release; both Parlophone and Nettwerk released it as a CD in 2000, and it was also released as a [Cassette](/wiki/Compact_cassette "Compact cassette") by American label [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") in 2001. In the following year, Parlophone issued the album as an [LP](/wiki/LP_record "LP record").[[35]](#cite_note-35) Four singles were released from *Parachutes*, including "Shiver" and "Yellow", and enjoyed popularity in the United Kingdom and United States.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The third single was "[Trouble](/wiki/Trouble_(Coldplay_song) "Trouble (Coldplay song)")", which reached number 10 on the UK Singles Chart.[[37]](#cite_note-37) It was released more than a year later in the United States and reached number 28 in the [Alternative Songs](/wiki/Alternative_Songs "Alternative Songs") chart.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In December 2001, the band released a limited-edition CD, *Mince Spies*, featuring a remix of "Yellow" and the Christmas song "[Have Yourself a Merry Little Christmas](/wiki/Have_Yourself_a_Merry_Little_Christmas "Have Yourself a Merry Little Christmas")". It was pressed to 1,000 copies and issued only to fans and journalists.[[39]](#cite_note-MOSS-39)

*Parachutes* was nominated for the [Mercury Music Prize](/wiki/Mercury_Music_Prize "Mercury Music Prize") in September 2000.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Having found success in Europe, the band targeted North America[[41]](#cite_note-41) by releasing the album there in November 2000, and started the US Club Tour in February 2001.[[42]](#cite_note-42) At the 2001 [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards") in February, Coldplay earned awards for [Best British Group](/wiki/BRIT_Award_for_British_Group "BRIT Award for British Group") and Best British Album.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Although *Parachutes* was a slow-burning success in the United States, it eventually reached double-platinum status.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The album was critically well received and earned a [Best Alternative Music Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Alternative_Music_Album "Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album") honours at the [2002 Grammy Awards](/wiki/2002_Grammy_Awards "2002 Grammy Awards").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Martin said the success was meant to make Coldplay the "biggest, best band in the world".[[46]](#cite_note-46) After single-handedly managing the band until early 2001, Harvey resigned due to the stress of having to perform duties that typically require a team of people. He became the group's creative director and is often referenced as their fifth member; Dave Holmes replaced him as manager.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### 2002–2004: *A Rush of Blood to the Head*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2002–2004: A Rush of Blood to the Head")]

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

["The Scientist"](/wiki/File:Coldplay_-_The_Scientist.ogg "File:Coldplay - The Scientist.ogg")

From the album *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")*, Martin wrote "[The Scientist](/wiki/The_Scientist_(song) "The Scientist (song)")" after listening to [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison")'s "[All Things Must Pass](/wiki/All_Things_Must_Pass_(song) "All Things Must Pass (song)")".

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

After the success of *Parachutes*, Coldplay returned to the studio in September 2001 to begin work on their second album, *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")*, once again with Ken Nelson producing. They had trouble focusing in London and relocated to Liverpool, where they recorded some of *Parachutes*. Once there, Martin said that they became obsessed with recording. "[In My Place](/wiki/In_My_Place "In My Place")" was the first song recorded for the album. The band released it as the album's lead single because it was the track that made them want to record a second album, following a "strange period of not really knowing what we were doing" three months after the success of *Parachutes*. According to Martin "one thing kept us going: recording 'In My Place'. Then other songs started coming".[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The band wrote more than 20 songs for the album. Some of their new material, including "In My Place" and "Animals", were played live while the band were still touring *Parachutes*.[[39]](#cite_note-MOSS-39)[[49]](#cite_note-49) The album's title was revealed through a post on the band's official website.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The album was released in August 2002 and spawned several successful singles, including "In My Place", "[Clocks](/wiki/Clocks_(song) "Clocks (song)")", and the ballad "[The Scientist](/wiki/The_Scientist_(song) "The Scientist (song)")", which was inspired by [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison")'s 1970 song "[All Things Must Pass](/wiki/All_Things_Must_Pass_(song) "All Things Must Pass (song)")".[[51]](#cite_note-51) During the week of the album's release, manager Phil Harvey quit the band, having struggled with the pressure that came with the band's early success, and went on to travel across South America and study in Australia.[[11]](#cite_note-TCR-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Chris_Martin_%2855208418%29.jpeg/250px-Chris_Martin_%2855208418%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Chris_Martin_(55208418).jpeg)

Martin performing with Coldplay in 2002

Coldplay toured from 19 June 2002 to 8 September 2003 for the [Rush of Blood to the Head Tour](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head_Tour "A Rush of Blood to the Head Tour"). They visited five continents, including co-headlining festival dates at [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival"),[[21]](#cite_note-ROACH28-21) [V2003](/wiki/V_Festival "V Festival") and [Rock Werchter](/wiki/Rock_Werchter "Rock Werchter"). Many concerts showcased elaborate lighting and individualised screens reminiscent of U2's [Elevation Tour](/wiki/Elevation_Tour "Elevation Tour") and [Nine Inch Nails](/wiki/Nine_Inch_Nails "Nine Inch Nails")' [Fragility Tour](/wiki/Fragility_Tour "Fragility Tour").[[52]](#cite_note-52) During the extended tour, Coldplay recorded a live DVD and CD, *[Live 2003](/wiki/Live_2003_(Coldplay_album) "Live 2003 (Coldplay album)")*, at Sydney's [Hordern Pavilion](/wiki/Hordern_Pavilion "Hordern Pavilion").[[53]](#cite_note-53) At the 2003 [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards") held at [Earls Court](/wiki/Earls_Court_Exhibition_Centre "Earls Court Exhibition Centre"), London, Coldplay received awards for Best British Group and Best British Album.[[54]](#cite_note-54) On 28 August 2003, Coldplay performed "The Scientist" at the [2003 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2003_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2003 MTV Video Music Awards") at the [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") in New York City and won three awards.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

In December 2003, readers of *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* magazine chose Coldplay as the best artist and the best band of the year.[[57]](#cite_note-57) At that time the band covered [the Pretenders](/wiki/The_Pretenders "The Pretenders")' 1983 song "[2000 Miles](/wiki/2000_Miles "2000 Miles")" (which was made available for download on their official website). "2000 Miles" was the top selling British download that year, with proceeds from the sales donated to Future Forests and [Stop Handgun Violence](/wiki/Stop_Handgun_Violence "Stop Handgun Violence") campaigns.[[58]](#cite_note-58) *A Rush of Blood to the Head* won the [Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Alternative_Music_Album "Grammy Award for Best Alternative Music Album") at the [2003 Grammy Awards](/wiki/2003_Grammy_Awards "2003 Grammy Awards").[[59]](#cite_note-59) At the [2004 Grammy Awards](/wiki/2004_Grammy_Awards "2004 Grammy Awards"), Coldplay earned [Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year") for "Clocks".[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### 2005–2007: *X&Y*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2005–2007: X&Y")]

In 2004, Coldplay released a satirical music video of a song from a fictional band, the Nappies, while recording their third album.[[61]](#cite_note-61) *[X&Y](/wiki/X%26Y "X&Y")* was released in June 2005 in the United Kingdom and Europe.[[62]](#cite_note-62) This new, delayed release date had put the album back into the next fiscal year, and the late release was blamed for a drop in EMI's stock.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) It became the best-selling album of 2005 with worldwide sales of 8.3 million.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The [lead single](/wiki/Lead_single "Lead single"), "[Speed of Sound](/wiki/Speed_of_Sound_(song) "Speed of Sound (song)")",[[66]](#cite_note-66) made its radio and online music store debut on 18 April and was released as a CD on 23 May 2005.[[67]](#cite_note-67) *X&Y* entered the album charts of 32 countries at the number one position[[68]](#cite_note-68) and was the third-fastest-selling album in United Kingdom history.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Following the album's release, Coldplay reinstated former manager Phil Harvey as a creative "[jack of all trades](/wiki/Jack_of_all_trades "Jack of all trades")", helping with creative elements such as designing live shows and producing music videos; he has since been credited as a full member of the band.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

[![Coldplay performing at Twisted Logic Tour](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Coldplay_in_Barcelona_2.jpg/250px-Coldplay_in_Barcelona_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_in_Barcelona_2.jpg)

Coldplay performing in Barcelona during [Twisted Logic Tour](/wiki/Twisted_Logic_Tour "Twisted Logic Tour") in 2005

Two other singles were released that year: "[Fix You](/wiki/Fix_You "Fix You")" in September and "[Talk](/wiki/Talk_(Coldplay_song) "Talk (Coldplay song)")" in December. Critical reaction to *X&Y* was mostly positive, though slightly less enthusiastic than that of its predecessor. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* critic Jon Pareles infamously described Coldplay as "the most insufferable band of the decade",[[71]](#cite_note-PARELES-71) whereas *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* awarded the album 9/10, calling it "confident, bold, ambitious, bunged with singles and impossible to contain *... X&Y* doesn't reinvent the wheel but it does reinforce Coldplay as the band of their time."[[72]](#cite_note-72) Comparisons to [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") became commonplace.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Martin said the critical review of the album by the *New York Times* made him feel liberated as he "agreed with a lot of the points", adding that "in a way, it was liberating to see that someone else realised that also".[[74]](#cite_note-74)

From June 2005 to March 2007, Coldplay went on their [Twisted Logic Tour](/wiki/Twisted_Logic_Tour "Twisted Logic Tour"), which included festival dates like [Coachella](/wiki/Coachella_Valley_Music_and_Arts_Festival "Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival"), [Isle of Wight Festival](/wiki/Isle_of_Wight_Festival_2006 "Isle of Wight Festival 2006"), [Glastonbury](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival") and the [Austin City Limits Music Festival](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits_Music_Festival "Austin City Limits Music Festival").[[75]](#cite_note-75) In July 2005, the band appeared at [Live 8](/wiki/Live_8 "Live 8") in [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_London "Hyde Park, London"), where they played a rendition of [the Verve](/wiki/The_Verve "The Verve")'s "[Bitter Sweet Symphony](/wiki/Bitter_Sweet_Symphony "Bitter Sweet Symphony")" with [Richard Ashcroft](/wiki/Richard_Ashcroft "Richard Ashcroft") on vocals.[[76]](#cite_note-76) On 28 August, Coldplay performed "Speed of Sound" at the [2005 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2005_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2005 MTV Video Music Awards") in Miami.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In September, Coldplay recorded a new version of "How You See the World" with reworked lyrics for [War Child](/wiki/War_Child_(charity) "War Child (charity)")'s *[Help!: A Day in the Life](/wiki/Help!:_A_Day_in_the_Life "Help!: A Day in the Life")* charity album.[[78]](#cite_note-78) In February 2006, Coldplay earned Best Album and Best Single honours at the [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards").[[79]](#cite_note-79) Three more singles were released during 2006 and 2007, "[The Hardest Part](/wiki/The_Hardest_Part_(Coldplay_song) "The Hardest Part (Coldplay song)")", "What If" and "White Shadows".

### 2008–2010: *Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2008–2010: Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")]

See also: [The Bakery](/wiki/The_Bakery_(recording_studio) "The Bakery (recording studio)"), [Million Dollar Maybe](/wiki/Million_Dollar_Maybe "Million Dollar Maybe"), and [Christmas Lights](/wiki/Christmas_Lights_(song) "Christmas Lights (song)")

In October 2006, Coldplay began work on their fourth studio album, *[Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")*, with producer [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno").[[80]](#cite_note-80) Taking a break from recording, the band toured Latin America in early 2007, finishing the Twisted Logic Tour while performing in Chile, Argentina, Brazil and Mexico.[[81]](#cite_note-81) After recording in churches and other venues in Latin America and Spain during their tour, the band said the album would likely reflect [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") influence.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The group then spent the rest of the year recording most of the album with Eno.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Martin described *Viva la Vida* as a new direction for Coldplay: a change from their past three albums, which the band felt was a "trilogy" that they had finished.[[84]](#cite_note-SERPICK-84) He said the album featured less of his [falsetto](/wiki/Falsetto "Falsetto") as he allowed his voice's lower register to take precedence.[[84]](#cite_note-SERPICK-84) Some songs, such as "[Violet Hill](/wiki/Violet_Hill_(song) "Violet Hill (song)")", contain distorted guitar [riffs](/wiki/Riff "Riff") and bluesy undertones.[[84]](#cite_note-SERPICK-84)

[![Coldplay's Viva la Vida Tour stage in Dallas](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Coldplay_Live_on_their_Viva_La_Vida_Tour_in_Dallas%2C_Texas.jpg/250px-Coldplay_Live_on_their_Viva_La_Vida_Tour_in_Dallas%2C_Texas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_Live_on_their_Viva_La_Vida_Tour_in_Dallas,_Texas.jpg)

Coldplay on stage in Dallas, Texas, during the [Viva la Vida Tour](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_Tour "Viva la Vida Tour")

"Violet Hill" was confirmed as the first single, with a radio release date of 29 April 2008.[[85]](#cite_note-85) After the first play, it was freely obtainable from Coldplay's website from 12:15 pm (GMT +0) for one week until it became commercially available on 6 May.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) "Violet Hill" entered the Top 10 in the United Kingdom and the Top 40 in the United States.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The title track, "[Viva la Vida](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida "Viva la Vida")", was also released exclusively on iTunes; it became the band's first number-one single on both the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100")[[90]](#cite_note-90) and UK Singles Chart.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Coldplay performed the song live for the first time at the [2008 MTV Movie Awards](/wiki/2008_MTV_Movie_Awards "2008 MTV Movie Awards") on 1 June.[[92]](#cite_note-92) "Viva la Vida" became iTunes' best-selling song of 2008.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

*Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends* topped the album charts worldwide and was the world's best-selling album of 2008.[[94]](#cite_note-94) It hit number one on the British albums chart despite having come on the market only three days previously. In that time, it sold 302,000 copies, being "one of the fastest-selling albums in the country's history".[[95]](#cite_note-95) By the end of June, it had set a new record for most-downloaded album ever.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97) In October 2008, Coldplay won two [*Q* Awards](/wiki/Q_Awards "Q Awards") for Best Album for *Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends* and Best Act in the World Today.[[98]](#cite_note-98) On 9 November, Coldplay were named the World's Best-selling Act of 2008 at the [World Music Awards](/wiki/2008_World_Music_Awards "2008 World Music Awards") in Monte Carlo.[[99]](#cite_note-99) They also picked up two other awards: World's Best-selling Rock Act and Great Britain's Best-selling Act.[[100]](#cite_note-100) The band followed up *Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends* with the *[Prospekt's March](/wiki/Prospekt%27s_March "Prospekt's March")* EP, which was released on 21 November 2008. The extended play features songs from the album sessions and was originally made available on its own, while the album got re-issued with all EP tracks included on a bonus disc. "[Life in Technicolor II](/wiki/Life_in_Technicolor_II "Life in Technicolor II")" was the only single released.

[![Coldplay performing at Viva la Vida Tour](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Coldplay_performs_for_Nissan_Live_Sets_2.jpg/250px-Coldplay_performs_for_Nissan_Live_Sets_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_performs_for_Nissan_Live_Sets_2.jpg)

Coldplay performing at New York's [Hammerstein Ballroom](/wiki/Hammerstein_Ballroom "Hammerstein Ballroom") in 2008

Coldplay began their [Viva la Vida Tour](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_Tour "Viva la Vida Tour") in June with a free concert at Brixton Academy in London.[[101]](#cite_note-101) This was followed two days later by a 45-minute performance that was broadcast live from outside [BBC Television Centre](/wiki/BBC_Television_Centre "BBC Television Centre").[[102]](#cite_note-102) Released in late 2008, "[Lost!](/wiki/Lost! "Lost!")" became the third single from the album, featuring a new version with [Jay-Z](/wiki/Jay-Z "Jay-Z") called "Lost+". After performing the opening set on 14 March 2009 for [Sound Relief](/wiki/Sound_Relief "Sound Relief") at the [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground"), Coldplay headlined a sold-out concert later that same night.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Sound Relief is a benefit concert for victims of the [Victorian Bushfire Crisis](/wiki/Black_Saturday_bushfires "Black Saturday bushfires") and the Queensland [Floods](/wiki/Flood "Flood").[[104]](#cite_note-104) On 4 December 2008, [Joe Satriani](/wiki/Joe_Satriani "Joe Satriani") filed a [copyright infringement](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") lawsuit against Coldplay in the [California Central District Court](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Central_District_of_California "United States District Court for the Central District of California").[[105]](#cite_note-105) Satriani claimed that "Viva la Vida" incorporates "substantial, original portions" of his song "If I Could Fly" from the album *[Is There Love in Space?](/wiki/Is_There_Love_in_Space%3F "Is There Love in Space?")* (2004).[[106]](#cite_note-106) Coldplay denied the allegation.[[107]](#cite_note-107) In 2009, "Viva la Vida" won a [Grammy Award for Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year") and [Best Pop Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocals](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Pop_Performance_by_a_Duo_or_Group_with_Vocals "Grammy Award for Best Pop Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocals").[[108]](#cite_note-108) The lawsuit was eventually dismissed, with the judge declaring that both the parties should bear their own trial costs.[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Coldplay were nominated for four awards at the [2009 Brit Awards](/wiki/2009_Brit_Awards "2009 Brit Awards"): British Group, British Live Act, British Single ("Viva la Vida") and British Album (*Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends*).[[111]](#cite_note-111) At the [51st Grammy Awards](/wiki/51st_Grammy_Awards "51st Grammy Awards") in the same year, Coldplay won three [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") in the categories for Song of Year for "Viva la Vida", Best Rock Album for *Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends*, and Best Vocal Pop Performance by a Duo or Group for "Viva la Vida".[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113) A live album titled *[LeftRightLeftRightLeft](/wiki/LeftRightLeftRightLeft "LeftRightLeftRightLeft")* was recorded at various shows during the tour, being released on their website as a free download on 15 May 2009 and given away at the remaining concerts of the Viva la Vida Tour.[[114]](#cite_note-114) In October 2009, Coldplay won Song of the Year for "Viva la Vida" at the [American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers](/wiki/American_Society_of_Composers,_Authors_and_Publishers "American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers") (ASCAP) Awards in London.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Two months later, *Rolling Stone* readers listed the band as the fourth-best musicians of the 2000s.[[116]](#cite_note-116) In August 2010, Coldplay extended their publishing deal with [Universal](/wiki/Universal_Music_Publishing_Group "Universal Music Publishing Group"); the company worked with the group since 2007, when it acquired their original publisher, [BMG](/wiki/Bertelsmann_Music_Group#BMG_Music_Publishing "Bertelsmann Music Group").[[117]](#cite_note-117) "[Christmas Lights](/wiki/Christmas_Lights_(song) "Christmas Lights (song)")" was released four months later. The song received very positive reviews, and the music video features a [cameo](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") from actor [Simon Pegg](/wiki/Simon_Pegg "Simon Pegg"), a close friend of Martin, who plays a violin-playing [Elvis impersonator](/wiki/Elvis_impersonator "Elvis impersonator") in the background.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

### 2011–2012: *Mylo Xyloto*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2011–2012: Mylo Xyloto")]

See also: [Xyloband](/wiki/Xyloband "Xyloband") and [2012 Summer Paralympics closing ceremony](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics_closing_ceremony "2012 Summer Paralympics closing ceremony")

[![Coldplay performing in Toronto](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Coldplay_Toronto_2011_Muchmusic.jpg/250px-Coldplay_Toronto_2011_Muchmusic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_Toronto_2011_Muchmusic.jpg)

Coldplay playing at [MuchMusic](/wiki/MuchMusic "MuchMusic") in Toronto, September 2011

The band finished recording their new album in mid-2011. When Martin and Champion were interviewed by BBC Radio and asked about the album's lyrical themes, Martin replied "It's about love, addiction, OCD, escape and working for someone you don't like".[[119]](#cite_note-119) When asked whether or not their fifth album would be out by the summer, Martin and Champion said that there was plenty of work to be done before releasing it. They confirmed several festival appearances before its release date, including a headlining spot at the 2011 [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival"),[[120]](#cite_note-120) [T in the Park](/wiki/T_in_the_Park "T in the Park"),[[121]](#cite_note-121) [Austin City Limits Music Festival](/wiki/Austin_City_Limits_Music_Festival "Austin City Limits Music Festival"),[[122]](#cite_note-122) [Rock in Rio](/wiki/Rock_in_Rio "Rock in Rio")[[123]](#cite_note-123) and [Lollapalooza](/wiki/Lollapalooza "Lollapalooza") festival.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

In an interview on 13 January 2011, Coldplay mentioned that two new songs would be included on their upcoming fifth album, "[Princess of China](/wiki/Princess_of_China "Princess of China")" and "[Every Teardrop Is a Waterfall](/wiki/Every_Teardrop_Is_a_Waterfall "Every Teardrop Is a Waterfall")".[[125]](#cite_note-125) In a February interview, Parlophone president Miles Leonard told [HitQuarters](/wiki/HitQuarters "HitQuarters") that the band were still in the studio working on the album and that he expected the final version would appear "towards the autumn of this year".[[126]](#cite_note-126) On 31 May 2011, Coldplay announced that "Every Teardrop Is a Waterfall" was the first single for the fifth album. It was released on 3 June 2011. The band presented five new songs at festivals during the summer of 2011, "[Charlie Brown](/wiki/Charlie_Brown_(Coldplay_song) "Charlie Brown (Coldplay song)")", "[Hurts Like Heaven](/wiki/Hurts_Like_Heaven "Hurts Like Heaven")", "Us Against the World", "Princess of China" and "[Major Minus](/wiki/Major_Minus "Major Minus")".[[127]](#cite_note-127)

On 12 August 2011, Coldplay announced via their official website that *[Mylo Xyloto](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto "Mylo Xyloto")* was the new album title and that it would be released on 24 October 2011.[[128]](#cite_note-128) On 12 September the band released "[Paradise](/wiki/Paradise_(Coldplay_song) "Paradise (Coldplay song)")", the second single from their upcoming album *Mylo Xyloto*. On 23 September 2011, tickets for Coldplay's European tour went on sale; the high demand led venues to be sold out in seconds.[[129]](#cite_note-129) *Mylo Xyloto* was released on 24 October 2011. It received positive reviews and topped the charts in over 34 countries.[[130]](#cite_note-MX1S-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)

[![Coldplay's Mylo Xyloto Tour lighting design](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Coldplay_Mylo_Xyloto_Tour_%40_Wells_Fargo_Center.jpg/250px-Coldplay_Mylo_Xyloto_Tour_%40_Wells_Fargo_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_Mylo_Xyloto_Tour_@_Wells_Fargo_Center.jpg)

The laser and light effects from the group's [Mylo Xyloto Tour](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto_Tour "Mylo Xyloto Tour")

On 19 October 2011, Coldplay performed songs at [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")'s private memorial for [Steve Jobs](/wiki/Steve_Jobs "Steve Jobs"), including "Viva la Vida", "Fix You", "Yellow" and "Every Teardrop Is a Waterfall".[[132]](#cite_note-132) On 26 October their "Amex Unstaged" concert at the [Plaza de Toros de Las Ventas](/wiki/Las_Ventas "Las Ventas") in Madrid, Spain, was streamed by YouTube as a live [webcast](/wiki/Webcast "Webcast") directed by [Anton Corbijn](/wiki/Anton_Corbijn "Anton Corbijn").[[133]](#cite_note-133) On 30 November 2011, Coldplay received three Grammy Award nominations for the [54th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/54th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "54th Annual Grammy Awards") which took place on 12 February 2012 in Los Angeles, and the band performed with [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") at the ceremony.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) On 12 January 2012, Coldplay were nominated for two [Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards "Brit Awards").[[136]](#cite_note-136) On 21 February 2012, they were awarded the Brit Award for Best British Group for the third time.[[137]](#cite_note-137) The album was the best-selling rock album in the United Kingdom, selling 908,000 copies.[[138]](#cite_note-RSOTY-138) The album's second single, "[Paradise](/wiki/Paradise_(Coldplay_song) "Paradise (Coldplay song)")", was also the best-selling rock single in the UK, selling 410,000 copies.[[138]](#cite_note-RSOTY-138) At the [2012 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2012_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2012 MTV Video Music Awards"), "Paradise" won [Best Rock Video](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Award_for_Best_Rock_Video "MTV Video Music Award for Best Rock Video").[[139]](#cite_note-139) *Mylo Xyloto* has sold over 8 million copies globally.[[130]](#cite_note-MX1S-130)

Coldplay headlined the [closing ceremony of the London 2012 Paralympic Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Paralympics_closing_ceremony "2012 Summer Paralympics closing ceremony") on 9 September 2012, where they performed alongside other artists including Rihanna and Jay-Z.[[140]](#cite_note-140) To tie in with their performance at the closing ceremony, the group gave permission for bands who were participating in the [Bandstand Marathon](/wiki/Bandstand_Marathon "Bandstand Marathon") the opportunity to perform their 2008 single "[Viva la Vida](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida "Viva la Vida")" to celebrate the end of the games.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

In October 2012, the music video for Coldplay's song "[Hurts Like Heaven](/wiki/Hurts_Like_Heaven "Hurts Like Heaven")" was released. The video was based on the story of Mylo Xyloto, a boy who grew up in tyranny run by Major Minus. The fictional comics titled *Mylo Xyloto* continued on the story portrayed in the music video when the series was released in early 2013. A concert documentary film and live album *[Live 2012](/wiki/Live_2012_(Coldplay_album) "Live 2012 (Coldplay album)")* chronicles their tour in support of the *Mylo Xyloto* album. The film premiered theatrically for one night only, 13 November 2012, and was released on CD and home video on 19 November 2012.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

On 21 November, after a concert in Brisbane, Australia as part of the group's [Mylo Xyloto Tour](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto_Tour "Mylo Xyloto Tour"), Coldplay hinted they were set to take a three-year break from touring.[[143]](#cite_note-143) They performed two shows with Jay-Z in the [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center"), Brooklyn, New York, on 30 December and New Year's Eve, which ended the Mylo Xyloto Tour. The Mylo Xyloto Tour was named the fourth-highest-grossing tour of 2012 worldwide with more than $171.3 million earned from ticket sales.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

### 2013–2014: *Ghost Stories*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2013–2014: Ghost Stories")]

In an interview from late 2012 with Australian radio station [2Day FM](/wiki/2Day_FM "2Day FM"), Martin revealed that the title for the band's next album would be "much easier to pronounce".[[145]](#cite_note-145) Martin debunked speculation that they were taking a break from touring by saying, "This three-year break idea only came about because I said at a gig in Australia that we might not be back there for three years. That's probably true, but that's just how a world tour works. No chance are we taking a three-year break".[[146]](#cite_note-146)

On 9 August 2013, Coldplay announced the release of "[Atlas](/wiki/Atlas_(Coldplay_song) "Atlas (Coldplay song)")", which was featured on the [soundtrack](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games:_Catching_Fire_%E2%80%93_Original_Motion_Picture_Soundtrack "The Hunger Games: Catching Fire – Original Motion Picture Soundtrack") for the film *[The Hunger Games: Catching Fire](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games:_Catching_Fire "The Hunger Games: Catching Fire")*.[[147]](#cite_note-147) Its release got pushed back to 6 September 2013 (everywhere but the UK) and 8 September (UK). In December 2013, it was announced that future Coldplay releases would be distributed by [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") in the United States due to the restructuring within [Warner Music](/wiki/Warner_Music_Group "Warner Music Group") following the purchase of [Parlophone](/wiki/Parlophone "Parlophone") from [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI").[[148]](#cite_note-148)

On 25 February 2014, the band unveiled "[Midnight](/wiki/Midnight_(Coldplay_song) "Midnight (Coldplay song)")", a track from their yet-to-be-released album.[[149]](#cite_note-149) In early March 2014, it was announced that the band's sixth album, *[Ghost Stories](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_(Coldplay_album) "Ghost Stories (Coldplay album)")*, would be released 19 May 2014.[[150]](#cite_note-150) According to Martin, *Ghost Stories* is a spiritually driven album that revolves around two major themes; it explores the idea of past actions and the effects they can have on your future and one's capacity for unconditional love.[[151]](#cite_note-151) The band took a different approach for their sixth studio album in contrast to their previous studio albums, with Martin inviting the band to contribute original songwriting material for the album, as opposed to building songs off his ideas as they had done during previous recording sessions.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

From April to July, Coldplay embarked on a six-date [Ghost Stories Tour](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_Tour "Ghost Stories Tour") in support of the album, playing "intimate" shows in six cities: the [Beacon Theatre](/wiki/Beacon_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Beacon Theatre (New York City)") in New York City on 5 May, [Royce Hall](/wiki/Royce_Hall "Royce Hall") in Los Angeles on 19 May, [Casino de Paris](/wiki/Casino_de_Paris "Casino de Paris") in Paris on 28 May, [Tokyo Dome City Hall](/wiki/Tokyo_Dome_City_Hall "Tokyo Dome City Hall") in Tokyo on 12 June, [Enmore Theatre](/wiki/Enmore_Theatre "Enmore Theatre") in Sydney on 19 June and finally [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") in London on 2 July 2014.[[153]](#cite_note-153) The album was made available for pre-order on [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes"), alongside new single "[Magic](/wiki/Magic_(Coldplay_song) "Magic (Coldplay song)")".[[154]](#cite_note-154) Two more singles from the album, "[A Sky Full of Stars](/wiki/A_Sky_Full_of_Stars "A Sky Full of Stars")" and "[True Love](/wiki/True_Love_(Coldplay_song) "True Love (Coldplay song)")", were then released. *Ghost Stories* received mixed to positive reviews.[[155]](#cite_note-155) The album topped the charts in the United Kingdom, the United States and most major markets.[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157) It received a Grammy Award nomination for [Best Pop Vocal Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Pop_Vocal_Album "Grammy Award for Best Pop Vocal Album"), and "A Sky Full of Stars" was nominated for [Best Pop Duo/Group Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Pop_Duo/Group_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Pop Duo/Group Performance").[[158]](#cite_note-158) In December 2014, [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") named Coldplay the most-streamed band in the world for 2014, as well as third most-streamed artist behind [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran") and [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem").[[159]](#cite_note-159)

### 2015–2018: *A Head Full of Dreams*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2015–2018: A Head Full of Dreams")]

See also: [Super Bowl 50 halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50_halftime_show "Super Bowl 50 halftime show")

On 4 December 2014, Martin announced in an interview with [Zane Lowe](/wiki/Zane_Lowe "Zane Lowe") on [BBC Radio 1](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1 "BBC Radio 1") that Coldplay were in the middle of working on their seventh studio album, *[A Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams "A Head Full of Dreams")*.[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161) Martin remarked it might be the band's final album and compared it to [Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter"): "It's our seventh thing, and the way we look at it, it's like the last Harry Potter book or something like that".[[162]](#cite_note-SA7-162) He added that unlike their promotion efforts for *Ghost Stories*, the band would tour for the seventh record.[[162]](#cite_note-SA7-162) In an interview with [Jo Whiley](/wiki/Jo_Whiley "Jo Whiley") on BBC Radio 2, Martin hinted at the style of the album by saying that the band were trying to make something colourful and uplifting yet not bombastic. He also stated that it would be something to "shuffle your feet" to.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

On 11 December 2014, the band unveiled a new song, "[Miracles](/wiki/Miracles_(Coldplay_song) "Miracles (Coldplay song)")", which was written and recorded for the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") drama film *[Unbroken](/wiki/Unbroken_(film) "Unbroken (film)")* directed by [Angelina Jolie](/wiki/Angelina_Jolie "Angelina Jolie").[[164]](#cite_note-164) At the [2015 Billboard Music Awards](/wiki/2015_Billboard_Music_Awards "2015 Billboard Music Awards") on 17 May, *Ghost Stories* was named Top Rock Album.[[165]](#cite_note-165) On 26 September, Coldplay performed at the 2015 [Global Citizen Festival](/wiki/Global_Citizen_Festival "Global Citizen Festival") in [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park")'s Great Lawn in New York, an event organised by Martin advocating for an end to extreme global poverty.[[166]](#cite_note-GCFEST-166) Coldplay, along with [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé"), [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran") and [Pearl Jam](/wiki/Pearl_Jam "Pearl Jam"), headlined the festival, which was broadcast on NBC in the United States on 27 September and the BBC in the United Kingdom on 28 September.[[166]](#cite_note-GCFEST-166)

Speaking on [Nick Grimshaw](/wiki/Nick_Grimshaw "Nick Grimshaw")'s *[Radio 1 Breakfast Show](/wiki/The_Radio_1_Breakfast_Show "The Radio 1 Breakfast Show")* on the BBC on 6 November, Coldplay confirmed 4 December as the release date of *A Head Full of Dreams*, and a new song from the album, "[Adventure of a Lifetime](/wiki/Adventure_of_a_Lifetime "Adventure of a Lifetime")", premiered on the show.[[167]](#cite_note-DREAMS-167) The album has guest appearances from Beyoncé, [Gwyneth Paltrow](/wiki/Gwyneth_Paltrow "Gwyneth Paltrow"), [Noel Gallagher](/wiki/Noel_Gallagher "Noel Gallagher"), [Tove Lo](/wiki/Tove_Lo "Tove Lo") and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama").[[167]](#cite_note-DREAMS-167) The album reached number one in the UK, and number two in the US, Australia and Canada among others where it was kept in second place by [Adele](/wiki/Adele "Adele")'s *[25](/wiki/25_(Adele_album) "25 (Adele album)")*.[[168]](#cite_note-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169) The music video for "Adventure of a Lifetime" featured the band performing as chimpanzees. They were provided consultation with renowned [performance capture](/wiki/Performance_capture "Performance capture") actor [Andy Serkis](/wiki/Andy_Serkis "Andy Serkis").[[170]](#cite_note-170)

[![Coldplay performing at Glastonbury 2016](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Coldplay_at_Glastonbury_2016_%283%29.jpg/250px-Coldplay_at_Glastonbury_2016_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_at_Glastonbury_2016_(3).jpg)

Coldplay performing "[Adventure of a Lifetime](/wiki/Adventure_of_a_Lifetime "Adventure of a Lifetime")" during their headline set at [Glastonbury 2016](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival_2016 "Glastonbury Festival 2016"). It was their sixth overall appearance at the festival and the fourth topping the bill.

On 27 November 2015, the first dates to the [Head Full of Dreams Tour](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour "A Head Full of Dreams Tour") were announced. Latin American and European stops were listed, which included three dates at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), London in June.[[171]](#cite_note-171) The North America Tour, an extra Wembley concert and an Oceania tour were later added. On 5 December, the band headlined the opening day of the 2015 [Jingle Bell Ball](/wiki/Jingle_Bell_Ball "Jingle Bell Ball") at London's [O2 Arena](/wiki/The_O2_Arena "The O2 Arena").[[172]](#cite_note-172) On 7 February 2016, they headlined the [Super Bowl 50 halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50_halftime_show "Super Bowl 50 halftime show")[[173]](#cite_note-173) and were joined by [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") and [Bruno Mars](/wiki/Bruno_Mars "Bruno Mars").[[174]](#cite_note-174) In April 2016, the band were named the sixth best-selling artist worldwide in 2015.[[175]](#cite_note-175)

On 26 June 2016, Coldplay closed the final day of the [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival") in England. Their performance included a duet with [Barry Gibb](/wiki/Barry_Gibb "Barry Gibb"), the last surviving member of the [Bee Gees](/wiki/Bee_Gees "Bee Gees").[[176]](#cite_note-176) During the band's second night at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") in New Jersey on 18 July, Coldplay were joined onstage by [Michael J. Fox](/wiki/Michael_J._Fox "Michael J. Fox") to recreate a *[Back to the Future](/wiki/Back_to_the_Future "Back to the Future")* scene. Martin sang "[Earth Angel](/wiki/Earth_Angel "Earth Angel")" before introducing Fox onstage to join the band in performing the Chuck Berry classic "[Johnny B. Goode](/wiki/Johnny_B._Goode "Johnny B. Goode")".[[177]](#cite_note-177)

The band performed a full set in India for the first time as part of the [Global Citizen Festival](/wiki/Global_Citizen_Festival "Global Citizen Festival") in [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") on 19 November 2016. This performance was attended by 80,000 people and also featured many Bollywood stars during the concert.[[178]](#cite_note-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179) The same month, Coldplay announced in interviews with [Absolute Radio](/wiki/Absolute_Radio "Absolute Radio") that they would be releasing new songs in a record called the *[Kaleidoscope EP](/wiki/Kaleidoscope_EP "Kaleidoscope EP")*. Described as a leftover "bag of ideas" from the recording of *A Head Full of Dreams*, Martin stated that it would be released in "a couple of months".[[180]](#cite_note-180)

[![Coldplay performing at A Head Full of Dreams Tour](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/ColdplayParis160717-7_%2835938478112%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-ColdplayParis160717-7_%2835938478112%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ColdplayParis160717-7_(35938478112)_(cropped).jpg)

Coldplay playing the second of three sold-out concerts at [Stade de France](/wiki/Stade_de_France "Stade de France") in Paris, July 2017

On 22 February 2017, the band released a collaboration track with EDM duo [The Chainsmokers](/wiki/The_Chainsmokers "The Chainsmokers"), called "[Something Just Like This](/wiki/Something_Just_Like_This "Something Just Like This")". Reaching number 2 on the UK Singles Chart and number 3 on the *Billboard* Hot 100, it was the lead single from the *Kaleidoscope EP*. They premiered the song live at the [2017 Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards_2017 "Brit Awards 2017"), with Martin also performing a tribute to [George Michael](/wiki/George_Michael "George Michael").[[181]](#cite_note-181) On 2 March, Martin's birthday, Coldplay released a new track from the EP, "[Hypnotised](/wiki/Hypnotised_(Coldplay_song) "Hypnotised (Coldplay song)")".[[182]](#cite_note-182) Two further releases from, "[All I Can Think About Is You](/wiki/All_I_Can_Think_About_Is_You "All I Can Think About Is You")" and "[Aliens](/wiki/Aliens_(song) "Aliens (song)")", came out on 15 June and 6 July respectively.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184)

On 8 October 2017, Coldplay debuted live their new song called "Life Is Beautiful" at [SDCCU Stadium](/wiki/San_Diego_Stadium "San Diego Stadium") in [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"). This part of the show was broadcast at the end of Estamos Unidos Mexicanos, a [benefit concert](/wiki/Benefit_concert "Benefit concert") at [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City")'s [Zócalo](/wiki/Z%C3%B3calo "Zócalo"). Proceeds from the song and concert were donated to relief efforts after the [Puebla earthquake](/wiki/2017_Puebla_earthquake "2017 Puebla earthquake").[[185]](#cite_note-185) On 15 November 2017, the Head Full of Dreams Tour finished with a total gross of $523 million from 5.38 million tickets sold, ranking as the [third-highest-grossing concert tour of all time](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_concert_tours "List of highest-grossing concert tours").[[186]](#cite_note-186) On 30 November 2018, Coldplay released *[Global Citizen – EP 1](/wiki/Global_Citizen_%E2%80%93_EP_1 "Global Citizen – EP 1")* under the name Los Unidades, with proceeds donated towards efforts to end global poverty.[[187]](#cite_note-187) Their fifth live album, *[Live in Buenos Aires](/wiki/Live_in_Buenos_Aires_(Coldplay_album) "Live in Buenos Aires (Coldplay album)")*, arrived on 7 December 2018 with footage from the final concert of the tour in [La Plata](/wiki/La_Plata "La Plata").[[188]](#cite_note-RS-188)

### 2019–2020: *Everyday Life*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2019–2020: Everyday Life")]

On 18 October 2019, mysterious black-and-white posters began appearing in numerous countries around the world, with Coldplay wearing vintage-style clothing and a date showing 22 November 1919. The band also changed their profile pictures on social media to a sun and moon, making fans speculate an imminent release of new material.[[189]](#cite_note-189) On 19 October 2019, a cryptic five-second teaser was released on social media with orchestral music in the background.[[190]](#cite_note-190) On 21 October 2019, in a letter sent to fans, the band announced that their eighth studio album would be titled *[Everyday Life](/wiki/Everyday_Life_(Coldplay_album) "Everyday Life (Coldplay album)")*. It serves as a double album, with the first half titled *Sunrise* and the second half titled *Sunset*.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

[![Coldplay performing in 2020](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/ALTer_EGO_1_18_2020_%2850740812871%29.jpg/250px-ALTer_EGO_1_18_2020_%2850740812871%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ALTer_EGO_1_18_2020_(50740812871).jpg)

Coldplay performing at the ALTer EGO concert in January 2020

On 23 October 2019, the album's track list was revealed on the advertisements from local newspapers in the United Kingdom; those included the *[Daily Post](/wiki/Daily_Post_(North_Wales) "Daily Post (North Wales)")* in North Wales (with whom Buckland once had a holiday job) and *[Express & Echo](/wiki/Express_%26_Echo "Express & Echo")* in Exeter (Martin's hometown).[[192]](#cite_note-192) Double lead singles "[Orphans](/wiki/Orphans_(Coldplay_song) "Orphans (Coldplay song)")" and "[Arabesque](/wiki/Arabesque_(Coldplay_song) "Arabesque (Coldplay song)")" were released on 24 October 2019 during the [Annie Mac](/wiki/Annie_Mac "Annie Mac") show on [BBC Radio 1](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1 "BBC Radio 1"). "Arabesque" was the first Coldplay song to feature [profanity](/wiki/Profanity "Profanity").[[193]](#cite_note-193) The album was released on 22 November 2019 and accompanied by a double concert at the [Amman Citadel](/wiki/Amman_Citadel "Amman Citadel") in Jordan.[[194]](#cite_note-194) The performance was broadcast live on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube"), being carried out at sunrise and sunset to match the album's two halves.[[195]](#cite_note-195) Inside the *Everyday Life* booklet, the words "Music of the Spheres" appeared written on a billboard.[[196]](#cite_note-196)

Martin had earlier said that the band would not tour to promote the album until they could work out "how our tour can not only be sustainable (but) how can it be actively beneficial", and hope that it would be entirely [carbon-neutral](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality").[[197]](#cite_note-197) However, Coldplay performed a one-off show on 25 November 2019 for the charity [ClientEarth](/wiki/ClientEarth "ClientEarth") at London's [Natural History Museum](/wiki/Natural_History_Museum,_London "Natural History Museum, London"). The band played beneath [Hope](/wiki/Natural_History_Museum,_London#Major_specimens_and_exhibits "Natural History Museum, London"), a giant 128-year-old skeleton of a blue whale in the museum's great hall.[[198]](#cite_note-198) The album debuted at number one on the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart") with 81,000 copies sold, making it the band's eighth consecutive number-one album. It was also the third fastest-selling album of 2019, behind *[No.6 Collaborations Project](/wiki/No.6_Collaborations_Project "No.6 Collaborations Project")* and *[Divinely Uninspired to a Hellish Extent](/wiki/Divinely_Uninspired_to_a_Hellish_Extent "Divinely Uninspired to a Hellish Extent")*.[[199]](#cite_note-199) On 24 November 2020, Coldplay received two nominations for the [63rd Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/63rd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "63rd Annual Grammy Awards"), with one of them being Album of the Year, their first nomination in the category since *Viva la Vida*.[[200]](#cite_note-200) On 21 December 2020, "Flags" was released internationally, the song was originally included as a Japanese bonus track of *Everyday Life*.[[201]](#cite_note-201)

### 2021–present: *Music of the Spheres* and *Moon Music*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2021–present: Music of the Spheres and Moon Music")]

See also: [Impact of the Music of the Spheres World Tour](/wiki/Impact_of_the_Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Impact of the Music of the Spheres World Tour")

On 29 April 2021, Coldplay announced "[Higher Power](/wiki/Higher_Power_(Coldplay_song) "Higher Power (Coldplay song)")" for release on 7 May 2021, coinciding with a video livestream from the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station").[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203) Martin stated in an interview with Lowe that the band would be working with producer [Max Martin](/wiki/Max_Martin "Max Martin") and his team on both the song and the new album. He said, "Max is our producer right now for everything we do".[[204]](#cite_note-204) On 4 May 2021, they were revealed as the opening act for the [2021 Brit Awards](/wiki/Brit_Awards_2021 "Brit Awards 2021"), where they performed "Higher Power" for the first time.[[205]](#cite_note-205)

[![Coldplay performing with Ed Sheeran in 2021](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/ColdplayShepBush121021_%2857_of_76%29_%2851582857133%29.jpg/250px-ColdplayShepBush121021_%2857_of_76%29_%2851582857133%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ColdplayShepBush121021_(57_of_76)_(51582857133).jpg)

Coldplay with [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran") at the [O2 Shepherd's Bush Empire](/wiki/Shepherd%27s_Bush_Empire "Shepherd's Bush Empire") in 2021

On 22 May 2021, the band participated in the *Live at Worth Farm* broadcast,[[206]](#cite_note-206) showcasing a new song called "Human Heart" with R&B duo [We Are King](/wiki/We_Are_King "We Are King").[[207]](#cite_note-207) On 8 June 2021, the official music video for "Higher Power", directed by Dave Meyers, premiered on YouTube, following a visualiser with Coldplay performing the song while dancing with [CGI](/wiki/Computer-generated_imagery "Computer-generated imagery") alien holograms that premiered on 7 May 2021.[[208]](#cite_note-208) On 20 July 2021, the group announced that their ninth album, *[Music of the Spheres](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_(Coldplay_album) "Music of the Spheres (Coldplay album)")*, would be released on 15 October 2021, and scheduled the closing track "[Coloratura](/wiki/Coloratura_(song) "Coloratura (song)")" for release on 23 July 2021 in anticipation for the project.[[209]](#cite_note-FLOYD-209)

On 13 September 2021, Coldplay announced that the album's second single, "[My Universe](/wiki/My_Universe_(song) "My Universe (song)")", a collaboration with [South Korean pop](/wiki/K-pop "K-pop") group [BTS](/wiki/BTS "BTS"), would be made available on 24 September 2021.[[210]](#cite_note-210) The song debuted at number three on the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart"), becoming their first Top 10 single in the country since "[Something Just Like This](/wiki/Something_Just_Like_This "Something Just Like This")" in 2017.[[211]](#cite_note-211) It also debuted at number one on the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100").[[212]](#cite_note-212) A short documentary about the collaboration was released on 26 September 2021 on the official BTS YouTube channel.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/ColdplayWembley160822_%2819_of_81%29_%2852291279744%29.jpg/250px-ColdplayWembley160822_%2819_of_81%29_%2852291279744%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ColdplayWembley160822_(19_of_81)_(52291279744).jpg)

Coldplay performing at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in August 2022 during the [Music of the Spheres World Tour](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Music of the Spheres World Tour")

*Music of the Spheres* went number one the [UK Albums Chart](/wiki/UK_Albums_Chart "UK Albums Chart") with 101,045 units sold,[[214]](#cite_note-214) becoming the fastest-selling record in the country since [Ed Sheeran](/wiki/Ed_Sheeran "Ed Sheeran")'s *[No.6 Collaborations Project](/wiki/No.6_Collaborations_Project "No.6 Collaborations Project")* (2019).[[215]](#cite_note-215) It debuted at number four on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart[[216]](#cite_note-216) and reached the top on both the [Top Alternative Albums](/wiki/Top_Alternative_Albums "Top Alternative Albums") and the [Top Rock Albums](/wiki/Top_Rock_Albums "Top Rock Albums") charts.[[217]](#cite_note-217) On 14 October 2021, Coldplay announced the [Music of the Spheres World Tour](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Music of the Spheres World Tour"), which began in [San José](/wiki/San_Jos%C3%A9,_Costa_Rica "San José, Costa Rica")'s [Estadio Nacional de Costa Rica](/wiki/Estadio_Nacional_de_Costa_Rica_(2011) "Estadio Nacional de Costa Rica (2011)") on 18 March 2022 and visited over 40 countries afterward.[[218]](#cite_note-218)

The concert run's announcement also included [a series of environmental plans](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour#Production "Music of the Spheres World Tour") developed in two years with help from sustainability experts; they were aimed at reducing [CO2 emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") by 50% in comparison to the [Head Full of Dreams Tour](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour "A Head Full of Dreams Tour").[[219]](#cite_note-219) The shows became a leading topic of news coverage in visited regions[[220]](#cite_note-220) and boosted local economies,[[221]](#cite_note-221) and Coldplay's discography experienced a resurgence in sales and streaming.[[222]](#cite_note-222) The band also managed to beat their initial [carbon footprint](/wiki/Carbon_footprint "Carbon footprint") target, reducing it by 59% as of June 2024.[[223]](#cite_note-223)

On 23 November 2021, "Higher Power" was nominated for [Best Pop Duo/Group Performance](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Pop_Duo/Group_Performance "Grammy Award for Best Pop Duo/Group Performance") at the [64th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/64th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "64th Annual Grammy Awards").[[224]](#cite_note-224) In December 2021, Martin said Coldplay would release three more albums until 2025 during an interview for [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), with one of them being "kind of a musical" while their last will be a "back to the basics" self-titled record.[[225]](#cite_note-225) He added, however, that the band would still be active with smaller releases and worldwide touring after 2025.[[226]](#cite_note-226) On 23 February 2022, the band released a piano version of "[Let Somebody Go](/wiki/Let_Somebody_Go "Let Somebody Go")" and a cover of [Kid Cudi](/wiki/Kid_Cudi "Kid Cudi")'s "[Day 'n' Nite](/wiki/Day_%27n%27_Nite "Day 'n' Nite")", as part of [Spotify Singles](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") series.[[227]](#cite_note-227) They later received three nominations at the [65th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/65th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "65th Annual Grammy Awards"), including Album of the Year and Best Pop Vocal Album.[[228]](#cite_note-228)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Coldplay_Glasto24_290624_%2826%29_%2853836754632%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Coldplay_Glasto24_290624_%2826%29_%2853836754632%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_Glasto24_290624_(26)_(53836754632)_(cropped).jpg)

Coldplay performing at [Glastonbury Festival 2024](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival_2024 "Glastonbury Festival 2024"). It marked their record fifth time headlining the event.

On 13 June 2024, Coldplay announced "[Feelslikeimfallinginlove](/wiki/Feelslikeimfallinginlove "Feelslikeimfallinginlove")" as the lead single for *[Moon Music](/wiki/Moon_Music "Moon Music")*.[[229]](#cite_note-229) Details about the album were shared through their social media, being scheduled for release on 4 October.[[230]](#cite_note-ASWAD-230) CD and vinyl pressings were crafted from recycled polycarbonate and PET-plastic bottles, respectively.[[230]](#cite_note-ASWAD-230) Two weeks later, the band headlined [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival") for the fifth time, extending the record set in June 2016.[[231]](#cite_note-231) Guests included [Victoria Canal](/wiki/Victoria_Canal "Victoria Canal"), [Michael J. Fox](/wiki/Michael_J._Fox "Michael J. Fox") and [Laura Mvula](/wiki/Laura_Mvula "Laura Mvula").[[232]](#cite_note-232) They also revealed "[We Pray](/wiki/We_Pray "We Pray")", featuring [Little Simz](/wiki/Little_Simz "Little Simz"), [Burna Boy](/wiki/Burna_Boy "Burna Boy"), [Elyanna](/wiki/Elyanna "Elyanna") and [Tini](/wiki/Tini_(singer) "Tini (singer)").[[233]](#cite_note-233) It was made available as the second single on 23 August.[[234]](#cite_note-234) During their stay in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") on the Music of the Spheres World Tour, the group premiered "Good Feelings", with [Ayra Starr](/wiki/Ayra_Starr "Ayra Starr").[[235]](#cite_note-235)

*Moon Music* reached number one in 16 countries,[[236]](#cite_note-236) outselling the rest of the Top 40 combined in the United Kingdom.[[237]](#cite_note-237) The album also topped the *Billboard* 200 in the United States, marking the first time a British group led both charts simultaneously since 2016.[[238]](#cite_note-PAINE-238) Its visual companion, named *A Film for the Future*, was released in January 2025.[[239]](#cite_note-239) Coldplay held two shows at the [Narendra Modi Stadium](/wiki/Narendra_Modi_Stadium "Narendra Modi Stadium") during the same month, earning [the largest stadium attendance of the 21st century](/wiki/List_of_most-attended_concerts "List of most-attended concerts") with more than 111,000 people each night.[[240]](#cite_note-240)

## Artistry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Artistry")]

### Creative process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Creative process")]

During an interview for *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")*, Berryman explained the band usually have a title and concept in mind before the music arrives, which serves to provide a "framework into which we can work thematically".[[241]](#cite_note-MOTSNMEI-241) Martin described their way of writing new songs as "a series of doors".[[242]](#cite_note-ELYTI-242) He brings initial ideas to Buckland, and the guitarist either disapproves or gives his input; the same happens from Buckland to Berryman, and then to Champion, allowing all band members to express themselves artistically.[[242]](#cite_note-ELYTI-242) However, this process is known to not always be linear, since tracks like "[Magic](/wiki/Magic_(Coldplay_song) "Magic (Coldplay song)")" and "[Adventure of a Lifetime](/wiki/Adventure_of_a_Lifetime "Adventure of a Lifetime")" started through the bass and guitar riffs from Berryman and Buckland, respectively.[[243]](#cite_note-243) Coldplay often switch instruments while in the studio as well.[[244]](#cite_note-244) When questioned about avoiding explicit language in lyrics, Champion affirmed that swear words are "extremely useful at times", but found that overusing them can diminish their impact.[[242]](#cite_note-ELYTI-242) Music critics suggest the band have a pattern, taking turns between "overt bids for mainstream success and more self-consciously artsy prestige pieces".[[245]](#cite_note-245) Buckland stated that knowing the "big [album] is coming allows us to go a lot smaller" and be more "insular about what music we make sense".[[241]](#cite_note-MOTSNMEI-241)

### Musical and lyrical style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Musical and lyrical style")]

Coldplay have explored many musical styles throughout their career, with their sound being considered [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"),[[246]](#cite_note-246) [alternative pop](/wiki/Alternative_pop "Alternative pop"),[[247]](#cite_note-247) [pop rock](/wiki/Pop_rock "Pop rock"),[[248]](#cite_note-248) [post-Britpop](/wiki/Post-Britpop "Post-Britpop"),[[249]](#cite_note-249) [soft rock](/wiki/Soft_rock "Soft rock")[[250]](#cite_note-250) and [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music").[[251]](#cite_note-251) After winning a [Grammy Award for Best Rock Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Rock_Album "Grammy Award for Best Rock Album") in 2009, Martin jokingly stated in his acceptance speech that they were "limestone rock" in comparison to "[hard rock](/wiki/Hard_rock "Hard rock")".[[252]](#cite_note-252) The extended plays released in 1998 and 1999 have characteristics of [dream pop](/wiki/Dream_pop "Dream pop"), setting them apart from future releases.[[253]](#cite_note-253) Their first studio album, *[Parachutes](/wiki/Parachutes_(Coldplay_album) "Parachutes (Coldplay album)")* (2000), was described as melodic pop which combined "bits of distorted guitar riffs and swishing percussion", being "exquisitely dark and artistically abrasive".[[254]](#cite_note-254) Berryman called it a "quiet, polite record",[[255]](#cite_note-TIOBE-255) while Champion has compared the lyrics to [Lou Reed](/wiki/Lou_Reed "Lou Reed")'s "[Perfect Day](/wiki/Perfect_Day_(Lou_Reed_song) "Perfect Day (Lou Reed song)")", as they are moody but with twists that imply optimism, ultimately making an album defined by the contrast between beautiful and happy messages and notoriously sad sounds.[[256]](#cite_note-256)
