On the other hand, 2002's *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")* is full of "plaintive strums, weary arpeggios and pained melodies", along with a sense of urgency and heartbreak.[[257]](#cite_note-BLEED-257) During an interview, Martin affirmed the title means "doing something on impulse".[[257]](#cite_note-BLEED-257) Music critics described it as larger,[[258]](#cite_note-258) darker and colder than its predecessor,[[259]](#cite_note-259) praising Coldplay for showing a newfound confidence as well.[[260]](#cite_note-260) This style was kept for their third album, *[X&Y](/wiki/X%26Y "X&Y")* (2005),[[261]](#cite_note-261) although now with the addition of electronic influences and extensive use of [synthesisers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"),[[262]](#cite_note-262) having a grander scale in terms of both sound and existential themes.[[263]](#cite_note-263) Craig McLean from *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* called it "the work of an increasingly driven, punchier band", describing the melodies as "heartfelt stuff, with thumping guitar lines and emotive piano".[[255]](#cite_note-TIOBE-255) Lyrics have been described as the ruminations on Martin's "doubts, fears, hopes, and loves", with words that "are earnest and vague, so listeners can identify with the underlying concepts in the songs".[[264]](#cite_note-264)

[![Champion resting his hands in a timpani, wearing a light blue shirt and purple scarf](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida_tour.jpg/250px-Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida_tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Will_Champion_-_Viva_la_Vida_tour.jpg)

Champion with the [timpani](/wiki/Timpani "Timpani") and [church bell](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") used in "[Viva la Vida](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida "Viva la Vida")"

With *[Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")* and the subsequent *[Prospekt's March](/wiki/Prospekt%27s_March "Prospekt's March")* (both released in 2008), Coldplay further diversified their style and explored new territory following the completion of what they saw as a trilogy of albums.[[84]](#cite_note-SERPICK-84) The band experimented with numerous different instruments,[[265]](#cite_note-265) including [church organs](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)"), [tack pianos](/wiki/Tack_piano "Tack piano"), [santoors](/wiki/Santoor "Santoor") and [orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), all while using more layered productions.[[266]](#cite_note-266) They also tried distinct song structures and vocal identities at the suggestion of producer [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno"),[[267]](#cite_note-CMRSI-267) drawing influences from Oriental, [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_music "Hispanic music"), [African](/wiki/Music_of_Africa "Music of Africa") and [Middle Eastern](/wiki/Middle_Eastern_music "Middle Eastern music") sounds.[[84]](#cite_note-SERPICK-84) The title track, "[Viva la Vida](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida "Viva la Vida")", is considered [baroque pop](/wiki/Baroque_pop "Baroque pop"), and fourth single "[Strawberry Swing](/wiki/Strawberry_Swing "Strawberry Swing")" was described as having [psychedelic](/wiki/Psychedelic_music "Psychedelic music") inspirations.[[268]](#cite_note-268) They dabbled in [shoegaze](/wiki/Shoegaze "Shoegaze") on hidden track "Chinese Sleep Chant" as well.[[269]](#cite_note-269) Lyrics are more universal in comparison to previous material, with the subject matter being more collective as the band "delves into love, life, war and death".[[270]](#cite_note-270) Martin commented the revolution motifs were inspired by [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo")'s *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables "Les Misérables")* (1862).[[271]](#cite_note-271)

Those themes, along with some of the oriental influences, remained in 2011's *[Mylo Xyloto](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto "Mylo Xyloto")*,[[272]](#cite_note-272) a [concept album](/wiki/Concept_album "Concept album") that follows the story of two characters in the style of a [rock opera](/wiki/Rock_opera "Rock opera").[[273]](#cite_note-MXNMER-273) It expanded the spectrum of Coldplay's sound by including more electronic elements than before and featuring mostly upbeat tones for the first time, resulting in a [pop rock](/wiki/Pop_rock "Pop rock") style with modern, urban and dance melodies.[[273]](#cite_note-MXNMER-273) According to Champion, the band originally wanted to make an acoustic record, so when "[Paradise](/wiki/Paradise_(Coldplay_song) "Paradise (Coldplay song)")" started to take shape, they decided to begin a separate electronic album. However, the two of them ultimately became a single body of work, with songs like "[Charlie Brown](/wiki/Charlie_Brown_(Coldplay_song) "Charlie Brown (Coldplay song)")" and "Us Against the World" getting reworked into their current versions.[[274]](#cite_note-274) Berryman declared they approached the project with confidence.[[273]](#cite_note-MXNMER-273) Lyrically, Martin said he was mostly inspired by old school American [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti"), the [White Rose](/wiki/White_Rose "White Rose") movement and "being able to speak out or follow your passion, even if everybody seems against it".[[275]](#cite_note-275) In 2013, a [comic book](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto_(comics) "Mylo Xyloto (comics)") based on the record's plot was released in partnership with [Mark Osborne](/wiki/Mark_Osborne_(filmmaker) "Mark Osborne (filmmaker)").[[276]](#cite_note-276)

For *[Ghost Stories](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_(Coldplay_album) "Ghost Stories (Coldplay album)")* (2014), Coldplay adopted a melancholic and somber style considered reminiscent of their debut,[[277]](#cite_note-277) while incorporating [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"),[[278]](#cite_note-278) [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B"),[[279]](#cite_note-279) [synth-pop](/wiki/Synth-pop "Synth-pop") and [ambient](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music") influences.[[280]](#cite_note-280) Its melodies are also noticeably darker and more minimalistic than *Mylo Xyloto*,[[281]](#cite_note-281) having sparse arrangements that reflect their desire to "keep a sense of space" without "being afraid of silence" or "layering too many sounds".[[282]](#cite_note-PAYNE-282) The project is considered a [break-up](/wiki/Break-up "Break-up") album as well,[[283]](#cite_note-283) exploring lyrically how past events in one's life (their ghosts) affect the present. Martin called it a journey of learning about unconditional love after he divorced from [Gwyneth Paltrow](/wiki/Gwyneth_Paltrow "Gwyneth Paltrow").[[282]](#cite_note-PAYNE-282) A year later, *[A Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams "A Head Full of Dreams")* was released with a similar style, but featuring bright and uplifting tones instead,[[284]](#cite_note-AHFODR-284) making contrast with its predecessor while introducing elements of [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") and [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk"), most notably in lead single "[Adventure of a Lifetime](/wiki/Adventure_of_a_Lifetime "Adventure of a Lifetime")".[[285]](#cite_note-285) In the lyrics, they worked on subjects of unity, dreaming, parenthood, forgiveness, healing, and thankfulness.[[284]](#cite_note-AHFODR-284)

In 2017, the band made *[Kaleidoscope EP](/wiki/Kaleidoscope_EP "Kaleidoscope EP")* available as a companion piece to the album.[[286]](#cite_note-KEPRS-286) It included a live version of "[Something Just Like This](/wiki/Something_Just_Like_This "Something Just Like This")", their [EDM](/wiki/Electronic_dance_music "Electronic dance music") collaboration with [the Chainsmokers](/wiki/The_Chainsmokers "The Chainsmokers"), and Brian Eno's returning production in "[Aliens](/wiki/Aliens_(song) "Aliens (song)")".[[286]](#cite_note-KEPRS-286) Meanwhile, tracks such as "[All I Can Think About Is You](/wiki/All_I_Can_Think_About_Is_You "All I Can Think About Is You")" and "[Hypnotised](/wiki/Hypnotised_(Coldplay_song) "Hypnotised (Coldplay song)")" mixed Coldplay's newfound pop style with their alternative rock roots,[[287]](#cite_note-287) setting the template for *[Everyday Life](/wiki/Everyday_Life_(Coldplay_album) "Everyday Life (Coldplay album)")* (2019) which saw a return to the experimentation and organic sounds of *Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends* while having new influences from [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") and [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") music.[[288]](#cite_note-288) Released as the lead single along with "[Orphans](/wiki/Orphans_(Coldplay_song) "Orphans (Coldplay song)")", the song "[Arabesque](/wiki/Arabesque_(Coldplay_song) "Arabesque (Coldplay song)")" drew from [jazz fusion](/wiki/Jazz_fusion "Jazz fusion") and [afrobeat](/wiki/Afrobeat "Afrobeat") inspirations.[[289]](#cite_note-289) The band continued their lyrical themes of positivity, equality, hope, legacy and humanity, but added loss, pain and commentaries on issues such as [racism](/wiki/Racism "Racism"), [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality "Police brutality"), [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control") and [refugee crisis](/wiki/Refugee_crisis "Refugee crisis"); it was also their first album to feature [profanity](/wiki/Profanity "Profanity").[[290]](#cite_note-DFTB-290)

This multi-style approach was similarly present in 2021's *[Music of the Spheres](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_(Coldplay_album) "Music of the Spheres (Coldplay album)")*, although leaning towards pop sounds.[[291]](#cite_note-291) According to Berryman, the record "was created with one eye on the live performances" and that "shaped the overall energy levels and song selections" for it.[[292]](#cite_note-292) Martin added he was inspired by the *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* franchise, which made him wonder what other artists could be like across the universe after watching the [Mos Eisley Cantina](/wiki/Mos_Eisley "Mos Eisley") band perform.[[293]](#cite_note-293) New musical influences included "Human Heart" (an [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") collaboration with R&B duo [We Are King](/wiki/We_Are_King "We Are King") and [Jacob Collier](/wiki/Jacob_Collier "Jacob Collier")) and "[Coloratura](/wiki/Coloratura_(song) "Coloratura (song)")" (a [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") ballad running at 10 minutes and 18 seconds, making it the longest song the band have ever released).[[294]](#cite_note-294) The phrase "Everyone is an alien somewhere" was frequently used to promote the project; Champion stated it is meant to get people to look at what brings them together instead of what keeps them apart, since "from another planet's perspective, we would be the aliens".[[295]](#cite_note-295)

### Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Influences")]

[![An old man wearing glasses and a dark blue shirt is interviewed](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Brian_Eno_2015.png/250px-Brian_Eno_2015.png)](/wiki/File:Brian_Eno_2015.png)

[Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno") influenced Coldplay's ambient and electronica sound

Coldplay's music has been compared to [a-ha](/wiki/A-ha "A-ha"), [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2"), [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)"), [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.") and [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead").[[296]](#cite_note-296) They acknowledged Scottish band [Travis](/wiki/Travis_(band) "Travis (band)") and American singer [Jeff Buckley](/wiki/Jeff_Buckley "Jeff Buckley") as major influences on their early material as well,[[297]](#cite_note-297) which was mostly produced by [Ken Nelson](/wiki/Ken_Nelson_(British_record_producer) "Ken Nelson (British record producer)").[[298]](#cite_note-298) Martin is known to be a fan of [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"),[[299]](#cite_note-299) mentioned "spending three years trying to sound like [Eddie Vedder](/wiki/Eddie_Vedder "Eddie Vedder")" before Buckley,[[267]](#cite_note-CMRSI-267) and commented listening to many hymns when he was young due to his religious upbringing. In 2021, he cited Belgian singer-songwriter [Stromae](/wiki/Stromae "Stromae") as another influence, affirming "He is one of our heroes you know, he is one of those people that comes along and completely inspires you all over again".[[300]](#cite_note-300)

Buckland, on the other hand, stated [the Stone Roses](/wiki/The_Stone_Roses "The Stone Roses") were one of the reasons why he learned to play guitar.[[301]](#cite_note-301) In 2020, he shared on social media playlists with some of his favourite tracks and artists from each decade, including [the Velvet Underground](/wiki/The_Velvet_Underground "The Velvet Underground"), [Carole King](/wiki/Carole_King "Carole King"), [Joy Division](/wiki/Joy_Division "Joy Division"), [Talking Heads](/wiki/Talking_Heads "Talking Heads"), [Kate Bush](/wiki/Kate_Bush "Kate Bush"), [Donna Summer](/wiki/Donna_Summer "Donna Summer"), [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk"), [Beastie Boys](/wiki/Beastie_Boys "Beastie Boys") and many others.[[302]](#cite_note-302) He said during an interview in the following year his favourite song of all time is "[Teardrop](/wiki/Teardrop_(song) "Teardrop (song)")" by [Massive Attack](/wiki/Massive_Attack "Massive Attack").[[303]](#cite_note-303) Meanwhile, Berryman is known to be inspired by artists like [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown"), [Marvin Gaye](/wiki/Marvin_Gaye "Marvin Gaye"), [Kool & the Gang](/wiki/Kool_%26_the_Gang "Kool & the Gang") and [the Funk Brothers](/wiki/The_Funk_Brothers "The Funk Brothers").[[304]](#cite_note-304) He added that his musical taste is difficult to condense, but he could not live without [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") or [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown").[[305]](#cite_note-305) As for Champion, he commented that playing violin and piano since he was eight years old gave him a different perspective on drums, an intrument he only learned after joining the band.[[306]](#cite_note-306) During his early years, he listened to [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits"), [Nick Cave](/wiki/Nick_Cave "Nick Cave") and [traditional Irish folk music](/wiki/Music_of_Ireland#Traditional_music "Music of Ireland").[[307]](#cite_note-307) He has named [Ginger Baker](/wiki/Ginger_Baker "Ginger Baker"), [Dave Grohl](/wiki/Dave_Grohl "Dave Grohl") and [John Bonham](/wiki/John_Bonham "John Bonham") among his favourite drummers.[[308]](#cite_note-308)

For *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")* (2002), they drew inspiration from [Echo & the Bunnymen](/wiki/Echo_%26_the_Bunnymen "Echo & the Bunnymen"),[[309]](#cite_note-309) [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison")[[310]](#cite_note-310) and [Muse](/wiki/Muse_(band) "Muse (band)").[[311]](#cite_note-311) Their third album, *[X&Y](/wiki/X%26Y "X&Y")* (2005), was particularly influenced by [Kraftwerk](/wiki/Kraftwerk "Kraftwerk") and [Depeche Mode](/wiki/Depeche_Mode "Depeche Mode").[[312]](#cite_note-TWISTEDREF-312) The song "Til Kingdom Come" was first written as a collaboration with [Johnny Cash](/wiki/Johnny_Cash "Johnny Cash") before he died.[[312]](#cite_note-TWISTEDREF-312) Aside from Nelson, Coldplay also worked with [Danton Supple](/wiki/Danton_Supple "Danton Supple") during recording sessions.[[313]](#cite_note-313) On *[Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")* (2008), the band moved towards [art rock](/wiki/Art_rock "Art rock") sensibilities, with inspirations including [My Bloody Valentine](/wiki/My_Bloody_Valentine_(band) "My Bloody Valentine (band)"), [Blur](/wiki/Blur_(band) "Blur (band)") and [Arcade Fire](/wiki/Arcade_Fire "Arcade Fire").[[314]](#cite_note-314) After partnering with [Brian Eno](/wiki/Brian_Eno "Brian Eno") and [Jon Hopkins](/wiki/Jon_Hopkins "Jon Hopkins"), they began to incorporate elements of [ambient music](/wiki/Ambient_music "Ambient music") and [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica") into compositions.[[315]](#cite_note-315) Both producers returned for *[Mylo Xyloto](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto "Mylo Xyloto")* (2011), with Eno having a more direct role in the songwriting process.[[316]](#cite_note-316)

In 2014, [Paul Epworth](/wiki/Paul_Epworth "Paul Epworth") contributed to *[Ghost Stories](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_(Coldplay_album) "Ghost Stories (Coldplay album)")*.[[317]](#cite_note-317) Producers [Tim Bergling](/wiki/Avicii "Avicii") and [Madeon](/wiki/Madeon "Madeon") were involved as well[[318]](#cite_note-318) which resulted in tracks like "[A Sky Full of Stars](/wiki/A_Sky_Full_of_Stars "A Sky Full of Stars")" having a more "danceable flavor".[[319]](#cite_note-319) Made available in the following year, *[A Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams "A Head Full of Dreams")* (2015) featured producing duo [Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(record_producers) "Stargate (record producers)").[[320]](#cite_note-320) Long-time partners of the band include [Davide Rossi](/wiki/Davide_Rossi "Davide Rossi"),[[321]](#cite_note-321) Bill Rakho, [Rik Simpson](/wiki/Rik_Simpson "Rik Simpson") and Dan Green.[[322]](#cite_note-DREAMTEAM-322) The last three are referred as The Dream Team on *[Everyday Life](/wiki/Everyday_Life_(Coldplay_album) "Everyday Life (Coldplay album)")* (2019) and all four have been working with Coldplay since *Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends*.[[322]](#cite_note-DREAMTEAM-322) For their ninth album, *[Music of the Spheres](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_(Coldplay_album) "Music of the Spheres (Coldplay album)")* (2021), the group invited Swedish producer [Max Martin](/wiki/Max_Martin "Max Martin").[[323]](#cite_note-323) The song "People of the Pride" has an introduction inspired by a [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") performance at the [Global Citizen Festival](/wiki/Global_Citizen_Festival "Global Citizen Festival"),[[324]](#cite_note-324) while promotional single "[Coloratura](/wiki/Coloratura_(song) "Coloratura (song)")" drew comparisons to [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd").[[209]](#cite_note-FLOYD-209)

### Live performances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Live performances")]

[![Stage lights being activated in a darkened stadium](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour%2C_Wembley_Stadium_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour%2C_Wembley_Stadium_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour,_Wembley_Stadium_(cropped).jpg)

Coldplay at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") during the [Head Full of Dreams Tour](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour "A Head Full of Dreams Tour") in 2016. The band also had an additional smaller stage where they performed an acoustic set.[[325]](#cite_note-325)

Coldplay have been said to "make sure each tour is its own dazzling, light-up spectacular".[[326]](#cite_note-LIBPR-326) Their concerts often use [fireworks](/wiki/Fireworks "Fireworks"), [lasers](/wiki/Laser "Laser") and [confetti cannons](/wiki/Confetti "Confetti").[[327]](#cite_note-327) Furthermore, *[Pollstar](/wiki/Pollstar "Pollstar")* stated they were the first artist to include [LED wristbands](/wiki/Xyloband "Xyloband") in live performances.[[328]](#cite_note-328) The group are widely credited with popularising the feature as well.[[329]](#cite_note-POWER-329) Writing a review of *[Live in Buenos Aires](/wiki/Live_in_Buenos_Aires_(Coldplay_album) "Live in Buenos Aires (Coldplay album)")* (2018), Sam Sodomsky from *[Pitchfork](/wiki/Pitchfork_(website) "Pitchfork (website)")* hailed them as "one of the 21st century's most enduring live acts".[[326]](#cite_note-LIBPR-326) Meanwhile, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*'s [Alexis Petridis](/wiki/Alexis_Petridis "Alexis Petridis") commented that the set lists used by the band were "a bullish reminder of how [they] got, and then stayed, huge".[[330]](#cite_note-330) Both reviewers also noted how Martin usually interacts with the audience between each song's performance.

For [Ghost Stories Tour](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_Tour "Ghost Stories Tour") (2014), however, Coldplay staged intimate sets at venues like [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall") and the [Beacon Theatre](/wiki/Beacon_Theatre_(New_York_City) "Beacon Theatre (New York City)").[[331]](#cite_note-331) The concerts made use of new features including a [laser harp](/wiki/Laser_harp "Laser harp") and the [reactable](/wiki/Reactable "Reactable").[[332]](#cite_note-332) A similar approach was taken for *[Everyday Life](/wiki/Everyday_Life_(Coldplay_album) "Everyday Life (Coldplay album)")* (2019) over their environmental concerns,[[333]](#cite_note-333) with the band playing small shows for charity and a special livestream at the [Amman Citadel](/wiki/Amman_Citadel "Amman Citadel") in Jordan.[[334]](#cite_note-334) When reviewing these shows, Dan Stubbs from *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* stated "on stage is where Coldplay come alive, and where they make the most sense".[[335]](#cite_note-JORDAN-335) In 2022, Champion said [the Flaming Lips](/wiki/The_Flaming_Lips "The Flaming Lips") were instrumental in shaping their idea of how entertaining a concert can be, as the American band's tours have "a sense of wonder and sense of fun which we really respond to".[[336]](#cite_note-336)

### Fashion and aesthetic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Fashion and aesthetic")]

While members of the band dress casually in their personal lives,[[337]](#cite_note-337) they have adopted multiple styles and visuals for each album's promotion.[[338]](#cite_note-HALL-338) Jon O'Brien from [the Recording Academy](/wiki/The_Recording_Academy "The Recording Academy") observed Coldplay's fashion sense did not "extend beyond the student staple of hoodies and corduroys" during initial years.[[339]](#cite_note-339) Martin often wore [Make Trade Fair](/wiki/Make_Trade_Fair "Make Trade Fair") shirts and had multiple versions of the institution's logo written on the back of his hand.[[340]](#cite_note-340) In the marketing campaign for *[X&Y](/wiki/X%26Y "X&Y")* (2005), which featured a representation of the [Baudot code](/wiki/Baudot_code "Baudot code") on its artwork,[[341]](#cite_note-341) the group wore black shirts, jackets, suits and pants, accompanied by white shoes.[[255]](#cite_note-TIOBE-255) After being questioned about it, Martin said there was a "great security" in feeling part of a team.[[255]](#cite_note-TIOBE-255) In 2007, they began to work with stylist Beth Fenton to make clothes fit the "universe" or the stories being told.[[342]](#cite_note-FENTON-342) They met her through [Stella McCartney](/wiki/Stella_McCartney "Stella McCartney") and Sara Jowett, who helped Coldplay to produce uniforms for *[Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends")* (2008).[[342]](#cite_note-FENTON-342) Inspirations included [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")' *[Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band](/wiki/Sgt._Pepper%27s_Lonely_Hearts_Club_Band "Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band")* (1967) and jackets worn by [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") armies.[[343]](#cite_note-343) The piece Martin used at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in 2009 was added to the [V&A Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum") permanent collection as well.[[344]](#cite_note-344) During recording sessions for *[Mylo Xyloto](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto "Mylo Xyloto")* (2011), the band drew influence from graffiti and designed instruments, cover artworks and stages with street artist Paris.[[345]](#cite_note-345) Conversely, Mila Fürstová's melancholic etchings were adopted for *[Ghost Stories](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_(Coldplay_album) "Ghost Stories (Coldplay album)")* (2014).[[346]](#cite_note-FURSTOVA-346) Non-customised outfits returned, but with darker tones.[[347]](#cite_note-347)

[![Coldplay onstage with their respective instruments](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Coldplay_-_Global-Citizen-Festival_Hamburg_14_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Coldplay_-_Global-Citizen-Festival_Hamburg_14_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coldplay_-_Global-Citizen-Festival_Hamburg_14_(cropped).jpg)

Coldplay's aesthetic for *[A Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams "A Head Full of Dreams")* (2015) reflected the bright, uplifting sounds on the album

Around the same period, Martin started to wear buttons from Love Button Global Movement, which became his signature accessory.[[348]](#cite_note-348) The organization is focused on transforming the world through individual and collaborative "acts of kindness".[[349]](#cite_note-349) Clothes made for *[A Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams "A Head Full of Dreams")* (2015) then included details based on [tie-dye](/wiki/Tie-dye "Tie-dye") and [kaleidoscope](/wiki/Kaleidoscope "Kaleidoscope") visuals reminiscent of the 1970s decade.[[350]](#cite_note-350) Berryman's jacket and shirts worn by Buckland and Champion also had the [flower of life](/wiki/Overlapping_circles_grid "Overlapping circles grid") as a reference to the album cover,[[351]](#cite_note-AHFODFILM-351) while Martin drew media attention for using custom-made [Air Jordans](/wiki/Air_Jordan "Air Jordan").[[352]](#cite_note-AJFOOT-352) Additionally, instruments were decorated with stickers, some of which featured the names of the band's children.[[351]](#cite_note-AHFODFILM-351) In 2016, Fenton stated Coldplay's pieces are typically sourced from [Rag & Bone](/wiki/Rag_%26_Bone "Rag & Bone") and [Levi's](/wiki/Levi_Strauss_%26_Co. "Levi Strauss & Co.").[[352]](#cite_note-AJFOOT-352) Three years later, a photograph of Buckland's great-grandfather inspired the old-fashioned aesthetic of *[Everyday Life](/wiki/Everyday_Life_(Coldplay_album) "Everyday Life (Coldplay album)")* (2019),[[353]](#cite_note-WHISKY-353) characterised as "a sepia-tinged 1919 jazz band".[[338]](#cite_note-HALL-338) Nods to the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") were prominently made as well.[[353]](#cite_note-WHISKY-353) To promote *[Music of the Spheres](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_(Coldplay_album) "Music of the Spheres (Coldplay album)")* (2021), they added a space motif to all items and photoshoots.[[338]](#cite_note-HALL-338) Wardrobe items were sourced by [Applied Art Forms](/wiki/Applied_Art_Forms "Applied Art Forms"), the fashion brand Berryman established in the previous year.[[354]](#cite_note-354) Each member chose the basic pieces by themselves, modifying them with embroidery or patches.[[355]](#cite_note-355)

## Achievements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Achievements")]

See also: [List of awards and nominations received by Coldplay](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Coldplay "List of awards and nominations received by Coldplay")

Coldplay are considered the most successful band of the 21st century.[[356]](#cite_note-356) With over 160 million records worldwide, they are one of the [best-selling artists of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists").[[357]](#cite_note-357) *[Parachutes](/wiki/Parachutes_(Coldplay_album) "Parachutes (Coldplay album)")* (2000), *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")* (2002) and *[X&Y](/wiki/X%26Y "X&Y")* (2005) have all been listed among the 50 [best-selling albums in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of best-selling albums in the United Kingdom"), marking the most appearances by a group in the ranking.[[358]](#cite_note-358) The latter was also the third-fastest-selling album in the country's history upon release.[[359]](#cite_note-359) In 2008, "[Viva la Vida](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida "Viva la Vida")" became the first song by a British act to top the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart") and [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") simultaneously since [Rod Stewart](/wiki/Rod_Stewart "Rod Stewart")'s "[Maggie May](/wiki/Maggie_May "Maggie May")" (1971).[[360]](#cite_note-360) Its [namesake album](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_or_Death_and_All_His_Friends "Viva la Vida or Death and All His Friends") earned the largest [digital sales](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") of the 2000s in the United States.[[361]](#cite_note-361) In 2013, Coldplay were ranked as the most influential British celebrities in the world on [*Forbes* Celebrity 100](/wiki/Forbes_Celebrity_100 "Forbes Celebrity 100").[[362]](#cite_note-362) They became the first band to surpass a billion streams on [Spotify](/wiki/Spotify "Spotify") the following year.[[363]](#cite_note-363) Their performance at the [Super Bowl 50 halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50_halftime_show "Super Bowl 50 halftime show") registered the highest audience ever among groups, helping to make them the most googled band of 2016.[[364]](#cite_note-364) During the same year, Coldplay headlined [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival "Glastonbury Festival") for the fourth time, a record for any musician.[[a]](#cite_note-366) In 2021, "[My Universe](/wiki/My_Universe_(song) "My Universe (song)")" became the first song by a British group to debut atop *Billboard* Hot 100.[[366]](#cite_note-367) They have earned $2.48 billion from touring.[[367]](#cite_note-368) Both the [Head Full of Dreams](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour "A Head Full of Dreams Tour") (2016–2017) and [Music of the Spheres](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Music of the Spheres World Tour") (2022–2025) tours rank among the [highest-grossing runs of all time](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_concert_tours "List of highest-grossing concert tours"),[[368]](#cite_note-369) with the latter also being the [most-attended](/wiki/List_of_most-attended_concert_tours "List of most-attended concert tours").[[369]](#cite_note-370) In 2024, Coldplay were named the most played group of the 21st century across British media.[[370]](#cite_note-371) They became the first to surpass 100 million [monthly listeners](/wiki/List_of_most-streamed_artists_on_Spotify "List of most-streamed artists on Spotify") on Spotify the following year.[[371]](#cite_note-372)

The band have received numerous accolades throughout their career as well, becoming the most awarded and nominated group of all time at the [Brit Awards](/wiki/The_Brit_Awards "The Brit Awards") (nine victories from 32 nominations).[[372]](#cite_note-373) They are the first act to ever win [British Album of the Year](/wiki/Brit_Award_for_British_Album_of_the_Year "Brit Award for British Album of the Year") three times and [British Group](/wiki/Brit_Award_for_British_Group "Brit Award for British Group") four times, scoring the most nominations for both categories.[[373]](#cite_note-374) Coldplay have also won seven [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") from 39 nominations, receiving [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year") and [Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year") once, while being nominated for the [Album of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Album of the Year") category three times.[[374]](#cite_note-375) In January 2009, they received a [NRJ Award of Honour](/wiki/NRJ_Music_Award "NRJ Music Award") in recognition of their career accomplishments and impact.[[375]](#cite_note-376) The band were then named Songwriters of the Year at the [ASCAP London Music Awards](/wiki/American_Society_of_Composers,_Authors_and_Publishers "American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers") in the following year,[[376]](#cite_note-377) having previously received the same honor from the [Ivor Novello Awards](/wiki/Ivor_Novello_Awards "Ivor Novello Awards") in 2003.[[377]](#cite_note-378) Their single "[Atlas](/wiki/Atlas_(Coldplay_song) "Atlas (Coldplay song)")", which was released as part of *[The Hunger Games: Catching Fire](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games:_Catching_Fire "The Hunger Games: Catching Fire")* (2013) soundtrack,[[378]](#cite_note-379) was nominated for the [19th Critics' Choice Awards](/wiki/19th_Critics%27_Choice_Awards "19th Critics' Choice Awards") and shortlisted at the [87th Academy Awards](/wiki/87th_Academy_Awards "87th Academy Awards").[[379]](#cite_note-380) In 2014, Coldplay were ranked as the sixth-most-awarded group of all time by [Fuse](/wiki/Fuse_(TV_channel) "Fuse (TV channel)").[[380]](#cite_note-381) Two years later, the band were chosen for [*NME* Awards](/wiki/NME_Awards "NME Awards")' Godlike Genius Award, which honours "careers of music icons who have been pioneers in the industry".[[381]](#cite_note-382) They have earned two [American Music Awards](/wiki/American_Music_Awards "American Music Awards"); nine [*Billboard* Music Awards](/wiki/Billboard_Music_Awards "Billboard Music Awards"); eight [MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/MTV_Video_Music_Awards "MTV Video Music Awards"); three [Juno Awards](/wiki/Juno_Award "Juno Award") and two silver prizes at the [Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity](/wiki/Cannes_Lions_International_Festival_of_Creativity "Cannes Lions International Festival of Creativity"). They have also established 15 *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")*, holding 10 of them. In May 2022, it was reported that the band's estimated combined wealth—without Harvey—exceeded £471 million, placing them among the [richest musicians on the planet](/wiki/List_of_music_artists_by_net_worth "List of music artists by net worth").[[382]](#cite_note-RICH-383)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Main article: [Cultural impact of Coldplay](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay "Cultural impact of Coldplay")

[![Coldplay preparing to bow on stage after performing](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/ColdplayCWB210323_%286_of_6%29.jpg/250px-ColdplayCWB210323_%286_of_6%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ColdplayCWB210323_(6_of_6).jpg)

Coldplay at [Estádio Couto Pereira](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Couto_Pereira "Estádio Couto Pereira"), [Curitiba](/wiki/Curitiba "Curitiba") in 2023

Coldplay are considered one of the most influential bands of the 21st century.[[383]](#cite_note-384) Writing for *[Firstpost](/wiki/Firstpost "Firstpost")*, Lakshmi Govindrajan Javeri commented they have "mastered the art of reinvention", consequently widening the "roster of artists inspired by them" and creating "a rich multi-genre legacy" which impacted [a variety of mainstream and indie musicians worldwide](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay#Creative_inspiration "Cultural impact of Coldplay").[[384]](#cite_note-385) Songs from the group have been [covered](/wiki/List_of_cover_versions_of_Coldplay_songs "List of cover versions of Coldplay songs") and sampled on many occasions as well.[[71]](#cite_note-PARELES-71) They were credited with bringing the sound of mainstream rock towards "something more gentle and melodic" and ushering into new forms of songwriting when "British music struggled to define itself".[[385]](#cite_note-NIERENBERG-386) [Neil McCormick](/wiki/Neil_McCormick "Neil McCormick") from *[The Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* stated that the band represent the evolution of rock into a brand new era,[[386]](#cite_note-387) while *[Afisha](/wiki/Afisha "Afisha")*'s Sergey Stepanov described them as the 21st century equivalent to [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles").[[387]](#cite_note-388) Publications have also frequently labelled Coldplay as successors to [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") as the biggest band in the world.[[b]](#cite_note-393)

Despite their popularity and impact, the group earned a reputation as [polarising cultural icons](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay#Public_image "Cultural impact of Coldplay"), since they have attracted criticism as much as praise from the public and the music critics throughout their career.[[392]](#cite_note-NELSON-394) Michael Baumann explained for *[The Ringer](/wiki/The_Ringer_(website) "The Ringer (website)")* that Coldplay's rise to fame coincided with a decade marked by "artistic snark and cynicism: hipsterism, the peak of [pop punk](/wiki/Pop-punk "Pop-punk")" and the new rebirth of [rockism](/wiki/Rockism_and_poptimism "Rockism and poptimism"), meaning that their "earnest, nonthreatening, and profoundly accessible" public image was established at "the worst time of the past 40 years to be those things".[[393]](#cite_note-395) In a piece for *The New York Times*, [Jon Pareles](/wiki/Jon_Pareles "Jon Pareles") named them "the most insufferable band of the decade".[[71]](#cite_note-PARELES-71) Conversely, Lanre Bakare told *[El País](/wiki/El_Pa%C3%ADs "El País")* that Martin is new kind of pop star who connects with fans emotionally.[[394]](#cite_note-396) In 2022, *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*' [Mark Beaumont](/wiki/Mark_Beaumont_(journalist) "Mark Beaumont (journalist)") defended that Coldplay regained respect and credibility in all fields by dedicating themselves to stadium spectacles.[[395]](#cite_note-397)

Felipe Branco Cruz from *[Veja](/wiki/Veja_(magazine) "Veja (magazine)")* credited them with reinventing the concept of arena rock by turning fans into protagonists of the concert instead of mere spectators,[[396]](#cite_note-CRUZ-398) which included making the use of [LED wristbands](/wiki/Xyloband "Xyloband") on live performances widespread.[[329]](#cite_note-POWER-329) The [Music of the Spheres World Tour](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Music of the Spheres World Tour") (2022–2025) has solidified the group as pioneers for sustainability in live entertainment, reducing [CO2 emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") by 59% in comparison to [their previous concert run](/wiki/A_Head_Full_of_Dreams_Tour "A Head Full of Dreams Tour").[[397]](#cite_note-399) They were listed as one of the most influential climate action leaders in the world by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* as well.[[398]](#cite_note-400) The [British Phonographic Industry](/wiki/British_Phonographic_Industry "British Phonographic Industry") (BPI) has attributed rises in British music exportation to Coldplay's international success on several occasions.[[399]](#cite_note-401) The band were also praised for having creative and "forward-thinking" marketing strategies, with their use of the internet as a tool for promotion being considered revolutionary in the early 21st century.[[400]](#cite_note-402) In 2016, *[Q](/wiki/Q_(magazine) "Q (magazine)")* ranked their first five albums among the best of the last 30 years.[[401]](#cite_note-403) The second, *[A Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head "A Rush of Blood to the Head")* (2002), was added to the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/List_of_200_Definitive_Albums_in_the_Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "List of 200 Definitive Albums in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") and *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone%27s_500_Greatest_Albums_of_All_Time "Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Albums of All Time")*'s definitive editorials.[[402]](#cite_note-404) As another result of their impact and legacy, the group have spawned various [tribute albums, events and acts](/wiki/Cultural_impact_of_Coldplay#Tribute_projects "Cultural impact of Coldplay").[[403]](#cite_note-405)

## Other activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Other activities")]

### Philanthropy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Philanthropy")]

Coldplay donate 10% from all of their profits to charity.[[404]](#cite_note-406) The fund is held in a bank account none of the band members can access.[[405]](#cite_note-407) They have endorsed over 30 organisations over the years,[[406]](#cite_note-408) including [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International"), [Migrant Offshore Aid Station](/wiki/Migrant_Offshore_Aid_Station "Migrant Offshore Aid Station") and [Global Citizen Festival](/wiki/Global_Citizen_Festival "Global Citizen Festival").[[407]](#cite_note-409) In 2003, an acoustic cover of the song "[2000 Miles](/wiki/2000_Miles "2000 Miles")" was issued to raise funds for [Stop Handgun Violence](/wiki/Stop_Handgun_Violence "Stop Handgun Violence") and Future Forests.[[408]](#cite_note-410) Two years later, they partnered with the [Make Poverty History](/wiki/Make_Poverty_History "Make Poverty History") movement and were featured in one of their television campaigns.[[409]](#cite_note-411) Coldplay have also auctioned significant memorabilia for [Kids Company](/wiki/Kids_Company "Kids Company") in 2009, including Martin's first guitar, the globe from the *[Parachutes](/wiki/Parachutes_(Coldplay_album) "Parachutes (Coldplay album)")* (2000) album cover and their [Viva la Vida Tour](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_Tour "Viva la Vida Tour") (2008–2010) wardrobe.[[410]](#cite_note-412) They additionally became patrons for [ClientEarth](/wiki/ClientEarth "ClientEarth").[[411]](#cite_note-413)

[![White and black plastic collection boat on a river](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/45/The_Ocean_Cleanup_Klang_River_interceptor.jpg/250px-The_Ocean_Cleanup_Klang_River_interceptor.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Ocean_Cleanup_Klang_River_interceptor.jpg)

[The Ocean Cleanup](/wiki/The_Ocean_Cleanup "The Ocean Cleanup")'s Neon Moon I interceptor was sponsored by Coldplay and deployed on the [Klang River](/wiki/Klang_River "Klang River")

The band performed a modified version of "A Message" (named "A Message 2010") at *[Hope for Haiti Now](/wiki/Hope_for_Haiti_Now "Hope for Haiti Now")*, a telethon which raised money for the victims of the [2010 Haiti earthquake](/wiki/2010_Haiti_earthquake "2010 Haiti earthquake").[[412]](#cite_note-414) Berryman mentioned that "You can make people aware of issues. It isn't very much effort for us at all, if it can help people, then we want to do it".[[413]](#cite_note-415) In 2012, Album Artists staged an exhibition with artworks from *[Mylo Xyloto](/wiki/Mylo_Xyloto "Mylo Xyloto")* (2011) era to raise £610,000 for Kids Company.[[414]](#cite_note-416) Two years later, Martin joined [Band Aid](/wiki/Band_Aid_(band) "Band Aid (band)") for a second time and provided vocals to a new version of "[Do They Know It's Christmas?](/wiki/Do_They_Know_It%27s_Christmas%3F "Do They Know It's Christmas?")", which focused on aiding the [Ebola crisis](/wiki/2014_Ebola_outbreaks "2014 Ebola outbreaks").[[415]](#cite_note-417)

In 2017, Coldplay made a donation of undisclosed value to the Centre for Cancer Immunology at [University of Southampton](/wiki/University_of_Southampton "University of Southampton"), the first and only centre dedicated to cancer immunology research in the United Kingdom.[[416]](#cite_note-SOTON-418) The organisation reunited cancer scientists worldwide to enable the development of clinical trials and lifesaving drugs.[[416]](#cite_note-SOTON-418) In the following year, the band contributed to the *Plastic Oceans Album* from [Artists' Project Earth](/wiki/Artists%27_Project_Earth "Artists' Project Earth"), which was released on 20 February at London's Ocean Plastics Crisis Summit to raise awareness and funds to counter plastic pollution.[[417]](#cite_note-419) Still in 2018, they made *[Global Citizen – EP 1](/wiki/Global_Citizen_%E2%80%93_EP_1 "Global Citizen – EP 1")* available under the pseudonym Los Unidades, with royalties being directed to the institution's endeavours of education and advocacy towards the end of [extreme poverty](/wiki/Extreme_poverty "Extreme poverty").[[418]](#cite_note-420)

In 2020, the music video of "[Trouble in Town](/wiki/Trouble_in_Town_(song) "Trouble in Town (song)")" was inspired by [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s *[Animal Farm](/wiki/Animal_Farm "Animal Farm")* (1944) and had all of its proceeds from streaming and publishing donated to [Innocence Project](/wiki/Innocence_Project "Innocence Project") and the African Children's Feeding Scheme.[[419]](#cite_note-421) Coldplay pledged support to [the Ocean Cleanup](/wiki/The_Ocean_Cleanup "The Ocean Cleanup") as well, sponsoring two watercrafts that collect plastic from polluted rivers in [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia").[[420]](#cite_note-422) According to *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*, the band donated more than £2.1 million to environmental causes through their J Van Mars Foundation in 2021.[[382]](#cite_note-RICH-383) As part of their efforts to make touring sustainable, they collaborated with One Tree Planted to fund a tree per ticket at the [Music of the Spheres World Tour](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Music of the Spheres World Tour").[[421]](#cite_note-423) Following their first Indonesia concert in 2023, Coldplay announced the renewal of their Ocean Cleanup alliance, with a new watercraft being deployed in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta").[[422]](#cite_note-424) In 2024, it was revealed that 10% of the proceeds from their [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") and [Craven Park](/wiki/Craven_Park,_Hull "Craven Park, Hull") performances would be donated to [Music Venue Trust](/wiki/Music_Venue_Trust "Music Venue Trust") next year, helping grassroots venues and artists in the United Kingdom.[[423]](#cite_note-425)

### Politics and activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Politics and activism")]

[![A man wearing a pink shirt sings with a microphone while holding a flag](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/ColdplayWembley120925-177_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-ColdplayWembley120925-177_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ColdplayWembley120925-177_(cropped).jpg)

Martin performing "People of the Pride" with the [rainbow flag](/wiki/Rainbow_flag "Rainbow flag")

The band have been vocal about [fair trade](/wiki/Fair_trade "Fair trade"), collecting more than 70,000 signatures for [Oxfam](/wiki/Oxfam "Oxfam")'s [Make Trade Fair](/wiki/Make_Trade_Fair "Make Trade Fair") initiative across the [Rush of Blood to the Head](/wiki/A_Rush_of_Blood_to_the_Head_Tour "A Rush of Blood to the Head Tour") (2002–2003) and [Twisted Logic](/wiki/Twisted_Logic_Tour "Twisted Logic Tour") (2005–2007) tours.[[424]](#cite_note-426) In 2003, Martin condemned the [invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq") during a concert for [Teenage Cancer Trust](/wiki/Teenage_Cancer_Trust "Teenage Cancer Trust") at the [Royal Albert Hall](/wiki/Royal_Albert_Hall "Royal Albert Hall"), encouraging attendees to sing against war.[[425]](#cite_note-427) He also endorsed [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") presidential candidate [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") while accepting honours at the [46th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/46th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "46th Annual Grammy Awards").[[426]](#cite_note-428) His wardrobe for the [Viva la Vida Tour](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida_Tour "Viva la Vida Tour") (2008–2010) included [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") armbands.[[427]](#cite_note-429) Coldplay pledged support to [Meat Free Mondays](/wiki/Meatless_Monday "Meatless Monday") as well. [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") established the campaign hoping to slow down climate change by having at least one meatless day per week.[[428]](#cite_note-430)

In 2011, the group promoted the song "[Freedom for Palestine](/wiki/Freedom_for_Palestine "Freedom for Palestine")" on social media and drew 12,000 comments in less than a day.[[429]](#cite_note-431) [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") later removed their publication because its content was reported as abusive by numerous users.[[430]](#cite_note-432) Coldplay have also refused to arrange concerts in Israel.[[431]](#cite_note-433) Between 2019 and 2024, they collaborated with Palestinian musicians such as Norah Shaqur,[[432]](#cite_note-434) [Le Trio Joubran](/wiki/Le_Trio_Joubran "Le Trio Joubran") and [Elyanna](/wiki/Elyanna "Elyanna").[[433]](#cite_note-435) Martin said that "We don't believe in oppression, occupation, terrorism or genocide".[[434]](#cite_note-436) In 2022, the band backed the [Mahsa Amini protests](/wiki/Mahsa_Amini_protests "Mahsa Amini protests") in Iran and covered "[Baraye](/wiki/Baraye "Baraye")" at [Estadio River Plate](/wiki/Estadio_Monumental_(Buenos_Aires) "Estadio Monumental (Buenos Aires)").[[435]](#cite_note-437)

Coldplay endorsed the [LGBTQ community](/wiki/LGBTQ_community "LGBTQ community") as well, sparking controversy with their [Super Bowl 50 halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_50_halftime_show "Super Bowl 50 halftime show").[[436]](#cite_note-438) Many [conservatives](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism") accused them of promoting a [gay agenda](/wiki/Gay_agenda "Gay agenda") when rainbow placards were flipped to form the sentence "Believe in love".[[437]](#cite_note-439) *[The Advocate](/wiki/The_Advocate_(magazine) "The Advocate (magazine)")* ranked the effort among the greatest queer moments of the event.[[438]](#cite_note-440) In 2021, they signed a public letter championing the [Equality Act](/wiki/Equality_Act_(United_States) "Equality Act (United States)").[[439]](#cite_note-441) Members of the [Malaysian Islamic Party](/wiki/Malaysian_Islamic_Party "Malaysian Islamic Party") tried to cancel a performance at [Bukit Jalil National Stadium](/wiki/Bukit_Jalil_National_Stadium "Bukit Jalil National Stadium") because Martin waves the [rainbow flag](/wiki/Rainbow_flag "Rainbow flag") every night.[[440]](#cite_note-442) On national politics, Coldplay voted Remain at the [2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum").[[441]](#cite_note-443) The band played at [One Love Manchester](/wiki/One_Love_Manchester "One Love Manchester") in the following year, helping raise funds for the [Manchester Arena bombing](/wiki/Manchester_Arena_bombing "Manchester Arena bombing") victims.[[442]](#cite_note-444) In 2025, they opposed the unrestricted use of artificial intelligence across Britain and pushed authorities to combat [ticket touting](/wiki/Ticket_resale "Ticket resale").[[443]](#cite_note-445) Songs like "Trouble in Town" and "Guns" denounce [racism](/wiki/Racism "Racism"), [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality "Police brutality") and [armed violence](/wiki/Gun_violence "Gun violence").[[290]](#cite_note-DFTB-290)

### Endorsements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coldplay&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Endorsements")]

Despite their worldwide popularity, Coldplay have remained notoriously protective of how their material appears in media.[[444]](#cite_note-SMLT-446) The band allow songs to be used in television shows and movies regularly, but most commercials are forbidden.[[445]](#cite_note-447) In 2002, *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* reported they turned down over $85 million in contracts from companies including [Gatorade](/wiki/Gatorade "Gatorade"), [Diet Coke](/wiki/Diet_Coke "Diet Coke") and [Gap](/wiki/Gap_Inc. "Gap Inc.").[[446]](#cite_note-448) Martin stated "We would not be able to live with ourselves if we sold the songs' meanings like that".[[444]](#cite_note-SMLT-446) The first time one of their songs were used for advertisements was with "[Viva la Vida](/wiki/Viva_la_Vida "Viva la Vida")" in 2008, the group signed a deal with [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") to promote the exclusive availability of the single on [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store").[[447]](#cite_note-449) In 2010, Martin appeared at the company's annual special event.[[448]](#cite_note-450) Following the death of [Steve Jobs](/wiki/Steve_Jobs "Steve Jobs"), Coldplay performed on his memorial service at the [Apple Campus](/wiki/Apple_Infinite_Loop "Apple Infinite Loop"), posthumously thanking his support.[[449]](#cite_note-451)

[![Coldplay floating in the air with their respective instruments, assisted by cables](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b2/A_Sky_Full_of_Stars_Target_AD_%282014%29.png/250px-A_Sky_Full_of_Stars_Target_AD_%282014%29.png)](/wiki/File:A_Sky_Full_of_Stars_Target_AD_(2014).png)

[Target](/wiki/Target_Corporation "Target Corporation") commercial where Coldplay promoted *[Ghost Stories](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_(Coldplay_album) "Ghost Stories (Coldplay album)")* (2014)

Six years later, they took part in a [Target](/wiki/Target_Corporation "Target Corporation") commercial which promoted the exclusive deluxe edition of *[Ghost Stories](/wiki/Ghost_Stories_(Coldplay_album) "Ghost Stories (Coldplay album)")* (2014).[[450]](#cite_note-452) Meanwhile, the music video for "[Adventure of a Lifetime](/wiki/Adventure_of_a_Lifetime "Adventure of a Lifetime")", which was directed by [Mat Whitecross](/wiki/Mat_Whitecross "Mat Whitecross") and recorded at [the Imaginarium](/wiki/The_Imaginarium "The Imaginarium"), had a [Beats](/wiki/Beats_Electronics "Beats Electronics") product placement.[[451]](#cite_note-453) The company was allowed to use some parts of the video on their commercials as a return for covering the budget. In 2018, filmmaker [Jon M. Chu](/wiki/Jon_M._Chu "Jon M. Chu") revealed he sent a letter directly to the band laying out all his reasons in order to get permission for using "[Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_(Coldplay_song) "Yellow (Coldplay song)")" in *[Crazy Rich Asians](/wiki/Crazy_Rich_Asians_(film) "Crazy Rich Asians (film)")*.[[452]](#cite_note-454) After it was granted, a Chinese-language cover was commissioned.

In 2021, Coldplay announced a major partnership with German multinational [BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW") as part of their efforts to make touring as sustainable as possible.[[453]](#cite_note-BMWHP-455) They commented the company's technology, which includes the first recyclable car batteries in the world, is able to power live performances almost entirely from renewable energy.[[454]](#cite_note-456) As part of the deal, the band contributed on the marketing of two electric cars from the company and licensed "[Higher Power](/wiki/Higher_Power_(Coldplay_song) "Higher Power (Coldplay song)")" for advertisements.[[453]](#cite_note-BMWHP-455) In 2022, Coldplay partnered with [DHL](/wiki/DHL "DHL") to reduce the logistic impact of the [Music of the Spheres World Tour](/wiki/Music_of_the_Spheres_World_Tour "Music of the Spheres World Tour"), which included the use of [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle") and [biofuel](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel") whenever possible.[[455]](#cite_note-457)

* [Guy Berryman](/wiki/Guy_Berryman "Guy Berryman") – bass guitar, keyboards, backing vocals (1997–present)
